events.text: The patient is a 62-year-old female named Margaret Yuen. She is of East Asian (Chinese-American) descent, born in San Francisco, CA. She is a retired high school chemistry teacher. She has a 20 pack-year smoking history (quit 12 years prior to diagnosis). She has a history of hypertension, well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus, and mild chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD, GOLD Stage I). Her ECOG performance status at presentation is 1.

At age 62 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIIB (T3N2M0, AJCC 8th edition) non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma (ICD-O histology code 8140/3), of the right upper lobe (ICD-O site code C34.1). Summary stage: Regional. The tumor measured 4.8 cm in greatest dimension with involvement of the ipsilateral mediastinal lymph nodes (stations 4R and 7) on PET-CT and endobronchial ultrasound-guided biopsy (EBUS). No distant metastases were identified at initial staging. Relevant biomarkers: EGFR exon 21 L858R point mutation (detected by PCR and confirmed by NGS); PD-L1 TPS 15% (22C3 assay); ALK IHC negative; ROS1 FISH negative. Confirmation: Positive histology via CT-guided core needle biopsy of the primary mass and EBUS-guided fine needle aspiration of mediastinal lymph node station 4R. Tumor Size: 48 mm. Pack-year history: 20 pack-years (former smoker, quit 12 years prior).

At age 62 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from CT-guided core needle biopsy of right upper lobe mass and concurrent EBUS-guided FNA of right paratracheal lymph node (station 4R). The primary tumor specimen showed invasive pulmonary adenocarcinoma, acinar-predominant pattern with focal lepidic component. Tumor cells exhibit moderate nuclear pleomorphism. Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 positive (strong, diffuse), Napsin-A positive, CK7 positive, CK20 negative, p40 negative, consistent with primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma. The lymph node FNA showed metastatic adenocarcinoma. Molecular reflexed to NGS. EGFR mutation testing by cobas PCR: L858R detected in exon 21. PD-L1 22C3 TPS: 15%. ALK D5F3 IHC: negative. ROS1 IHC: negative (confirmed by FISH).

At age 62 years, the patient had a PET-CT (whole body with contrast CT), which showed a hypermetabolic 4.8 cm right upper lobe mass (SUVmax 14.2) with spiculated margins abutting the right upper lobe bronchus. Hypermetabolic right paratracheal (4R, SUVmax 8.7) and subcarinal (station 7, SUVmax 6.4) lymphadenopathy, consistent with N2 nodal involvement. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. No pleural effusion. Adrenal glands unremarkable. Brain not included (dedicated brain MRI ordered separately). Findings consistent with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) disease.

At age 62 years, the patient had an MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast (dedicated staging MRI), which showed no intracranial metastases. Normal brain parenchyma. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No mass lesion identified. Findings: No evidence of brain metastases at initial staging.

At age 62 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for non-small cell lung carcinoma (adenocarcinoma) based on a specimen obtained from the right upper lobe primary tumor (core needle biopsy, FFPE). Sequencing platform: 500-gene solid tumor panel (hybrid capture, tumor-only). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 6.2 mutations/megabase (low). Microsatellite instability (MSI): stable. Actionable alterations: EGFR p.L858R (exon 21, VAF 42%), classified as a sensitizing mutation with established benefit from EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) therapy. Co-mutations: TP53 p.R248W (VAF 38%, likely pathogenic); STK11 wild-type; KEAP1 wild-type; RB1 wild-type; SMAD4 wild-type. Copy number alterations: MET copy number 2 (no amplification); ERBB2 copy number 2 (no amplification); CDK6 copy number gain (low-level). Fusions: None detected (ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK1/2/3, MET exon 14 splice: all negative). Additional variants of uncertain significance: ARID1A p.E1814* (VAF 18%); KDR p.V297I (VAF 22%). No EGFR exon 19 deletion, T790M, exon 20 insertion, or other EGFR resistance mutations detected.

At age 62 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment at initial staging. Thoracic surgery evaluated the patient and felt the tumor was unresectable due to multi-station N2 disease (stations 4R and 7). Radiation oncology evaluated for definitive chemoradiation but given the presence of an actionable EGFR L858R mutation (sensitizing), the team discussed the emerging role of EGFR TKI-based approaches in Stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC. The MDT recommended systemic therapy with osimertinib as first-line treatment, given the LAURA trial data supporting osimertinib maintenance post-chemoradiation in EGFR-mutant Stage III NSCLC. However, the patient declined concurrent chemoradiation due to concern about toxicity given her underlying COPD. After extensive shared decision-making, the patient elected to proceed with upfront osimertinib monotherapy. Cancer present. Performance status: ECOG 1.

At age 62 years, the patient received curative/definitive-intent osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as first-line systemic therapy for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma. Treatment initiated approximately 6 weeks after diagnosis.

At age 62 years (approximately 3 months after starting osimertinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (first restaging), which showed significant partial response. The right upper lobe primary mass decreased from 4.8 cm to 2.6 cm (46% decrease in longest diameter). Right paratracheal and subcarinal lymph nodes decreased in size (4R: 1.8 cm to 0.9 cm; station 7: 1.5 cm to 0.7 cm). No new lesions. No pleural effusion. Findings: Partial response per RECIST 1.1. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 62 years (approximately 2 months after starting osimertinib), the patient developed a grade 2 acneiform rash (maculopapular eruption involving the face and upper trunk) consistent with EGFR TKI-related dermatologic toxicity. Managed with topical clindamycin 1% gel, oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily, and moisturizer regimen. Rash improved to grade 1 within 3 weeks without dose reduction. Additionally, grade 1 paronychia involving bilateral first toes was noted.

At age 62 years (approximately 4 months after starting osimertinib), the patient developed grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools per day above baseline), managed with dietary modification and loperamide as needed. No dose interruption required. Also noted: grade 1 dry eye syndrome managed with lubricating eye drops.

At age 62 years (6 months after starting osimertinib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. She reported significantly improved dyspnea and resolution of her initial presenting cough. ECOG performance status improved to 0. Physical examination unremarkable. Grade 1 residual acneiform rash being managed. Restaging imaging confirmed ongoing partial response. Labs: CBC and CMP within normal limits. HbA1c 6.9% (diabetes well-controlled). Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 62 years (approximately 9 months after starting osimertinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (second restaging), which showed continued partial response. Right upper lobe primary mass now 1.8 cm (from 2.6 cm at 3 months; 62% decrease from baseline). Right paratracheal lymph node 0.6 cm (no longer meets size criteria for pathologic enlargement). Subcarinal lymph node 0.5 cm. No new lesions. Liver, adrenal glands, and bones unremarkable. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 63 years (approximately 14 months after starting osimertinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (third restaging) and concurrent MRI brain with gadolinium, which showed disease progression. CT chest: Right upper lobe mass increased from 1.8 cm to 3.2 cm (78% increase from nadir). New right paratracheal lymphadenopathy (1.4 cm, previously normalized). New small right pleural effusion. MRI brain: Two new enhancing lesions identified -- a 1.1 cm lesion in the right frontal lobe and a 0.8 cm lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere, consistent with brain metastases. Findings: Progressive disease per RECIST 1.1 with new intracranial metastases. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 63 years (14 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had an urgent oncologist office assessment following imaging demonstrating systemic and intracranial progression. She reported worsening dyspnea over the past 3-4 weeks, a new mild bifrontal headache (no focal neurologic deficits on examination), and fatigue (ECOG PS 1). Brain MRI confirmed two new enhancing brain metastases. Plan: Obtain liquid biopsy (ctDNA) to assess for resistance mechanisms, specifically T790M mutation. Refer to radiation oncology for consideration of stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to brain metastases. Multidisciplinary discussion regarding next-line systemic therapy. Continue osimertinib during evaluation period. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 63 years, the patient had a pathology/molecular result of type LIQUID BIOPSY / CELL-FREE DNA ANALYSIS. Plasma ctDNA was obtained via peripheral blood draw (Guardant360 platform). Findings: EGFR L858R confirmed (VAF 3.8%). EGFR T790M detected (VAF 1.6%), confirming acquired resistance to osimertinib via the classic T790M mechanism. No MET amplification detected. No EGFR exon 20 C797S detected. No ERBB2 amplification. No BRAF V600E. TP53 R248W confirmed (VAF 5.2%). Findings: Acquired EGFR T790M resistance mutation confirmed on liquid biopsy, consistent with resistance to first-generation/second-generation EGFR TKIs; however, as the patient was treated with third-generation osimertinib upfront, the T790M likely represents a pre-existing subclonal population enriched under selective pressure, or possibly a distinct resistance mechanism pathway. This finding will influence the choice of subsequent therapy.

At age 63 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for non-small cell lung carcinoma (adenocarcinoma, recurrent/progressing) based on a specimen obtained from plasma (cell-free DNA, liquid biopsy, Guardant360 platform, 74-gene panel). Actionable alterations: EGFR p.L858R (VAF 3.8%, sensitizing mutation); EGFR p.T790M (VAF 1.6%, acquired resistance mutation to 1st/2nd gen EGFR TKIs; atypical in osimertinib-treated patient, possible pre-existing subclone). No EGFR exon 20 C797S detected (ruling out triple-mutant resistance). Co-mutations: TP53 p.R248W (VAF 5.2%); ARID1A frameshift (VAF 0.9%). Copy number alterations: No MET amplification; No ERBB2 amplification; No FGFR1 amplification. Fusions: None detected. TMB: low (estimated from ctDNA). Clinical interpretation: Acquired T790M in context of upfront osimertinib is uncommon. The treating team suspected concurrent bypass track mechanisms. Tissue re-biopsy discussed but deferred given planned SRS and clinical urgency. Next-line therapy to include consideration of amivantamab-lazertinib, platinum-based chemotherapy, or clinical trial enrollment.

At age 63 years (approximately 15 months after initial diagnosis, approximately 1 month after progression documented), the patient received stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to two brain metastases using a frameless linear accelerator-based system (Varian Edge). Right frontal lobe lesion (1.1 cm): 20 Gy in 1 fraction prescribed to the 80% isodose line. Right cerebellar hemisphere lesion (0.8 cm): 22 Gy in 1 fraction. Treatment was well-tolerated. No whole brain radiation therapy (WBRT) administered, consistent with modern management to preserve neurocognitive function. Radiation oncology plan: follow with MRI brain every 8-10 weeks.

At age 63 years (approximately 2 weeks post-SRS), the patient developed mild radiation-related fatigue (grade 1) and a transient grade 1 headache lasting approximately 5 days post-procedure, managed with acetaminophen and a short course of dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily (tapered over 7 days). No new neurologic deficits. No seizure activity.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 weeks after progression documented, following completion of SRS), the patient discontinued osimertinib and initiated second-line systemic therapy with carboplatin (AUC 5, day 1) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2, day 1) every 21 days for 4-6 cycles, with bevacizumab (15 mg/kg, day 1) added given the absence of squamous histology and manageable EGFR TKI-related toxicity history. This carboplatin/pemetrexed/bevacizumab regimen was selected as standard-of-care platinum-based chemotherapy for EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-TKI progression, given the patient's good performance status (ECOG 1) and adequate organ function. Folic acid supplementation and vitamin B12 injections initiated prior to pemetrexed.

At age 63 years (cycle 2 of carboplatin/pemetrexed/bevacizumab), the patient developed grade 2 nausea and vomiting, managed with ondansetron 8 mg every 8 hours and dexamethasone pre-medication optimization. Grade 2 fatigue noted (functional but with reduced activity level). CBC: Grade 1 anemia (hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL), managed with iron supplementation and dietary counseling. No neutropenia or thrombocytopenia requiring dose modification. Bevacizumab continued without hypertension or proteinuria issues.

At age 63 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed/bevacizumab, approximately 10 weeks after starting second-line therapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain, which showed a mixed response. CT chest: Right upper lobe mass decreased from 3.2 cm to 2.4 cm (25% decrease from the progression nadir). Right paratracheal lymphadenopathy decreased (1.4 cm to 0.9 cm). Right pleural effusion resolved. MRI brain: Right frontal SRS-treated lesion: 0.6 cm (decreased from 1.1 cm, consistent with treatment response/radiation necrosis). Right cerebellar SRS-treated lesion: 0.4 cm (decreased from 0.8 cm). No new intracranial lesions. Findings: Partial response in chest; brain metastases responding post-SRS. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 63 years (after 3 cycles of second-line carboplatin/pemetrexed/bevacizumab), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. She reported improved energy (ECOG 0-1), resolution of dyspnea, and stable headaches (not worsened). Imaging showed partial response systemically and intracranial response post-SRS. Plan to complete 4-6 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed/bevacizumab, then transition to pemetrexed/bevacizumab maintenance per standard of care. Patient and family counseled on clinical trial options. Research coordinator introduced to discuss available trials including MARIPOSA-2 extension cohort, and KRYSTAL-7 amivantamab-based trial. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 63 years (after completing 4 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed/bevacizumab with confirmed partial response), the patient transitioned to maintenance therapy with pemetrexed (500 mg/m2 every 21 days) plus bevacizumab (15 mg/kg every 21 days) per standard NSCLC maintenance approach. This maintenance phase was initiated approximately 5 months after the start of second-line therapy.

At age 63 years (approximately 7 months after starting second-line therapy, 2 months into maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (restaging on maintenance), which showed stable disease. Right upper lobe mass: 2.2 cm (stable from 2.4 cm, no significant change). Lymphadenopathy: stable subcentimeter nodes. No new lesions. No new pleural effusion. Liver and adrenal glands unremarkable. Findings: Stable disease per RECIST 1.1. Cancer present, neither responding nor progressing.

At age 63 years (during maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab, cycle 3 of maintenance), the patient developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (numbness and tingling in bilateral feet) likely related to prior carboplatin exposure. Neurology consulted. Managed with duloxetine 30 mg daily titrated to 60 mg daily. No dose modification required for pemetrexed or bevacizumab. Grade 1 alopecia (partial) noted. Blood pressure monitoring: mild hypertension (grade 1) on bevacizumab, managed with addition of amlodipine 5 mg daily to existing antihypertensive regimen.

At age 63 years (approximately 9 months after second-line therapy initiation, 4 months into maintenance), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. She reported manageable toxicities (grade 1-2 peripheral neuropathy, grade 1 fatigue). ECOG PS 1. Discussed clinical trial enrollment options with patient and her daughter (who attended the appointment). Patient expressed interest in trials targeting EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-TKI and post-chemotherapy progression. Research team presented eligibility criteria for a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating a novel bispecific antibody targeting EGFR and MET (investigational agent) in combination with a checkpoint inhibitor in EGFR-mutant NSCLC patients who progressed on prior EGFR TKI and platinum-based chemotherapy. Patient met preliminary eligibility criteria. Screening procedures initiated. Cancer present, stable.

At age 64 years (approximately 11 months after second-line therapy initiation, 6 months into maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain (routine restaging), which showed disease progression. CT chest: Right upper lobe mass increased from 2.2 cm to 3.8 cm (73% increase from maintenance nadir). New bilateral pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 0.8 cm, left lower lobe 0.6 cm), concerning for intrapulmonary metastases. MRI brain: Right frontal treated lesion: 1.4 cm with surrounding edema, increased from 0.6 cm -- concern for radiation necrosis versus local recurrence. No new untreated brain metastases. Findings: Progressive disease per RECIST 1.1. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 64 years (at the time of documented second progression), the patient had an urgent oncologist office assessment. She reported worsening dyspnea over 3 weeks, increased fatigue, and recurrent right-sided headaches. ECOG PS 2. Maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab discontinued. Brain MRI concerning for enlarging right frontal lesion -- neurosurgery and radiation oncology consulted; MRI perfusion and MR spectroscopy ordered to differentiate radiation necrosis from tumor recurrence. Clinical trial eligibility re-confirmed: patient has completed the required platinum-based chemotherapy exposure and remains EGFR L858R-positive. Decision made to proceed with enrollment on the Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating EGFR/MET bispecific antibody plus anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 64 years, the patient had an MRI brain with perfusion sequences and MR spectroscopy (dedicated post-SRS evaluation), which showed the right frontal lesion with elevated rCBV on perfusion imaging and elevated choline:NAA ratio on spectroscopy, favoring tumor recurrence over radiation necrosis. The neurosurgical team recommended stereotactic biopsy versus repeat SRS versus surgical resection. Given the patient's declining performance status (ECOG 2) and impending clinical trial enrollment, the multidisciplinary team elected to proceed with repeat SRS to the right frontal lesion prior to trial initiation. Cancer present, progressing (intracranial).

At age 64 years, the patient received repeat stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to the recurrent right frontal lobe lesion (1.4 cm) using Varian Edge linear accelerator. Dose: 18 Gy in 1 fraction (reduced from initial SRS dose given prior irradiation of the same volume, per radiation oncology recommendation). The cerebellar lesion remained stable/resolved and did not require repeat treatment. Procedure was well-tolerated. Patient started on dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for peritumoral edema, with plan to taper over 3 weeks.

At age 64 years (approximately 17 months after initial diagnosis, following second progression on maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab and after repeat SRS to brain), the patient was enrolled on a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody (study drug: ABBV-CLS-484 in combination with a novel anti-PD-L1 agent, NCT hypothetical). Tumor re-biopsy of the right upper lobe was performed to confirm EGFR L858R and assess for additional resistance mutations prior to trial enrollment. Patient initiated trial treatment with cycle 1, day 1 dosing. All study drug administered per protocol. Patient signed informed consent with her daughter present.

At age 64 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from CT-guided core needle biopsy of right upper lobe mass (repeat biopsy for clinical trial enrollment). The specimen showed invasive pulmonary adenocarcinoma, acinar-predominant pattern, consistent with prior diagnosis. No small cell transformation identified. No squamous differentiation. TTF-1 positive. PD-L1 TPS: 28% (22C3 assay, increased from initial 15%). Molecular testing reflexed to NGS for resistance profiling prior to trial enrollment.

At age 64 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for non-small cell lung carcinoma (adenocarcinoma, second progression/pre-clinical trial biopsy) based on a specimen obtained from right upper lobe primary tumor (CT-guided core biopsy, FFPE). Sequencing platform: Foundation One CDx (comprehensive genomic profiling, 324 genes). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 8.4 mutations/megabase (intermediate-low). Microsatellite instability (MSI): stable. Actionable alterations: EGFR p.L858R (VAF 48%, sensitizing mutation confirmed); EGFR p.T790M (VAF 22%, acquired resistance, now higher VAF in tissue vs. prior ctDNA); MET amplification detected (copy number 6.8, high-level amplification -- MET amplification as a bypass resistance mechanism to osimertinib confirmed). No EGFR exon 20 C797S. No KRAS mutation (consistent with EGFR-mutant NSCLC biology). Co-mutations: TP53 p.R248W (VAF 52%); RB1 p.Q670* nonsense mutation (VAF 18%, newly detected, potentially associated with accelerated resistance); ARID1A p.E1814* (VAF 24%, previously seen as VUS, now higher VAF). Copy number alterations: MET amplification (high-level, CN 6.8) -- primary acquired resistance mechanism; CDK4 amplification (low-level); CCNE1 amplification (low-level). Fusions: None detected (ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK1/2/3 all negative). Genomic interpretation: EGFR L858R/T790M co-mutation with high-level MET amplification as a combined resistance mechanism. RB1 loss potentially indicates evolving resistance biology. MET amplification is a targetable co-alteration relevant to EGFR/MET bispecific antibody trial.

At age 64 years (approximately 4 weeks after initiating clinical trial therapy), the patient had an oncologist office assessment (cycle 1, week 4 on-trial assessment). She tolerated the first cycle of investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody combination. Reported grade 1 fatigue and grade 1 infusion-related reaction (managed with pre-medications per protocol going forward). ECOG PS 2 (stable). Headaches improved following repeat SRS and dexamethasone taper now complete. Dyspnea slightly improved subjectively. Trial-required ECG and echocardiogram within normal limits. Next restaging imaging scheduled at cycle 3 (approximately 6 weeks from treatment initiation). Patient and family remain engaged in care; social work involved for support services given patient's functional decline from ECOG 0 at best response to ECOG 2. Cancer present, response to current therapy not yet assessable (too early).

documents.text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE
Hematology/Oncology | Thoracic Malignancies Program

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
FELLOW: [Fellow]
PATIENT: Margaret Yuen
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Visit Type: Established Patient Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Follow-up for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma, on osi (Tagrisso) monotherapy. Patient presents approximately 6 months after initiation of systemic therapy for routine reassessment and review of restaging imaging.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Yuen is a 62-year-old Chinese-American woman, retired high school chemistry teacher, with a history of Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) non-small cell lung cancer, acinar-predominant pulmonary adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, harboring an EGFR exon 21 L858R sensitizing mutation. She has a 20 pack-year smoking history and quit approximately 12 years prior to her cancer diagnosis. Relevant comorbidities include well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and mild COPD (GOLD Stage I).

She was diagnosed approximately 6 months ago following evaluation for progressive exertional dyspnea and a persistent productive cough. Initial staging workup demonstrated a 4.8 cm spiculated right upper lobe mass with hypermetabolic bilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 4R and 7 (SUVmax 8.7 and 6.4, respectively) on PET-CT, without evidence of distant metastatic disease. Brain MRI at staging was negative for intracranial metastases. CT-guided biopsy of the primary mass confirmed invasive adenocarcinoma (TTF-1+, Napsin-A+, acinar-predominant), with concurrent EBUS-guided FNA of station 4R confirming nodal metastatic disease. Molecular profiling by NGS revealed EGFR L858R (VAF 42%), co-mutations in TP53 R248W (VAF 38%), and a low-level CDK6 copy number gain. No ALK, ROS1, or other targetable fusions were identified. PD-L1 TPS was 15% by 22C3. TMB was 6.2 mut/Mb (low). MSI-stable.

The patient was evaluated by thoracic surgery (felt unresectable given multi-station N2 nodal involvement) and radiation oncology. At multidisciplinary tumor board, the team discussed the standard-of-care approach of concurrent chemoradiation followed by durva consolidation (PACIFIC regimen), as well as emerging data from the LAURA trial supporting Tagrisso as consolidation therapy following definitive chemoradiation in EGFR-mutant Stage III disease. The patient declined concurrent chemoradiation, citing concerns about pulmonary toxicity in the setting of her underlying COPD. After thorough discussion of risks and benefits, she elected to proceed with upfront osi monotherapy at 80 mg orally once daily, initiated approximately 6 weeks after diagnosis.

Since starting Tagrisso, she has had one interim restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast at approximately 3 months, which demonstrated a meaningful partial response: the primary RUL mass decreased from 4.8 cm to 2.6 cm (approximately 46% reduction), and mediastinal lymph nodes significantly decreased in size (station 4R: 1.8 cm to 0.9 cm; station 7: 1.5 cm to 0.7 cm). No new lesions were identified at that restaging.

Today she presents for her 6-month follow-up. She reports that her shortness of breath has improved dramatically -- she is now able to walk her dog around the neighborhood, which she was unable to do at the time of diagnosis. Her initial productive cough has largely resolved; she has only occasional dry cough, which she attributes to the Tagrisso. She denies hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, or worsening respiratory symptoms. She reports good energy overall and has resumed some of her volunteer tutoring activities.

Regarding toxicities: she developed a grade 2 acneiform rash involving her face and upper trunk approximately 2 months into therapy. This was treated with topical clindamycin 1% gel, oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily, and an intensive moisturizer regimen, with improvement to grade 1 within about 3 weeks; no dose reduction was required. She continues to have a grade 1 residual papular eruption on her chin and forehead, which is manageable and not distressing to her. She also notes mild paronychia of the bilateral great toes -- she has been keeping them dry and using topical antibiotic ointment. At approximately 4 months, she developed grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools per day above her baseline), managed with dietary modification and as-needed loperamide; this has largely resolved. She endorses mild dry eyes, managed with preservative-free artificial tears (Refresh Optive) applied two to three times daily.

She denies chest pain, palpitations, or syncope. She denies new headaches, vision changes, focal neurologic symptoms, or new bone pain. She denies lower extremity swelling.

She is up to date on her medications. She is adherent to Tagrisso, and states she takes it at the same time every evening with a small snack. She denies any missed doses in the past month.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Improved energy, no significant weight loss. No fevers or chills.
Respiratory: Dyspnea markedly improved from baseline. Occasional mild dry cough. No hemoptysis. No pleuritic pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
Neurologic: No headaches, no focal deficits, no vision changes, no memory concerns.
Gastrointestinal: Grade 1 diarrhea largely resolved. Good appetite. No nausea or vomiting.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain.
Dermatologic: Grade 1 acneiform rash (face/forehead/chin). Grade 1 paronychia, bilateral great toes.
Ophthalmologic: Grade 1 dry eye, managed with eye drops.
Endocrine: No symptoms of hyper- or hypoglycemia. Compliant with diabetes medications.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished Chinese-American woman. Alert and oriented x3. In no acute distress. Conversational and pleasant.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg (left arm, seated), heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius, weight [on file], height [on file].
ECOG Performance Status: 0 (from 1 at diagnosis; patient reports she is functioning at her normal baseline and above).
Head and Neck: Normocephalic. Atraumatic. Mild scattered papules on the forehead and chin with post-inflammatory erythema consistent with resolving acneiform eruption, grade 1. Sclerae anicteric. Pupils equal and reactive to light. No supraclavicular or anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. No lymphadenopathy in the neck. Oropharynx moist and without lesions.
Respiratory: Respiratory effort is unlabored. Chest is symmetrical. Dullness to percussion is absent bilaterally. On auscultation, breath sounds are clear bilaterally with very mild end-expiratory wheeze in the left base, consistent with known mild COPD; no new focal consolidation or crackles. No pleural friction rub.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normal bowel sounds. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. Bilateral great toe paronychia noted -- erythema and mild periungual swelling, non-purulent, non-fluctuant, consistent with grade 1 EGFR TKI-related paronychia. Capillary refill brisk bilaterally.
Neurologic: Alert and fully oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Coordination intact. Gait steady.
Skin: Papules on forehead and chin as described above. No new rash on upper trunk. No petechiae or ecchymoses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
Complete Blood Count (CBC):
  - WBC: 5.8 x10^3/uL (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 12.6 g/dL (mild decrease from 13.2 g/dL at baseline, stable over past 2 assessments)
  - Platelets: 214 x10^3/uL (normal)
  - Neutrophil count: 3.4 x10^3/uL (within normal limits)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (CMP):
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  - Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
  - Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
  - BUN: 16 mg/dL
  - Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL
  - ALT: 28 U/L (within normal limits)
  - AST: 24 U/L (within normal limits)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 72 U/L (normal)
  - Albumin: 3.9 g/dL

HbA1c: 6.9% (diabetes well-controlled; stable from 7.0% at prior visit)
Fasting glucose: 112 mg/dL
Magnesium: 1.9 mEq/L (lower limit of normal; will monitor)
Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH): 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
Lipid panel: Not repeated this visit (last checked 4 months ago, within normal limits).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (performed within the past 2 weeks, reviewed today):
The right upper lobe mass has continued to decrease in size. Compared to the prior CT at 3 months (mass was 2.6 cm), the current study shows the mass now measures approximately 1.8 cm in greatest dimension -- representing roughly a 62% overall reduction from the 4.8 cm at baseline. The mass retains a small residual spiculated component but has markedly diminished in bulk. The right paratracheal lymph node at station 4R measures 0.6 cm in short axis, essentially normalizing since its prior measurement of 0.9 cm at first restaging. The subcarinal node (station 7) measures 0.5 cm, down from 0.7 cm. No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No pleural effusion. The liver, adrenal glands, and visualized bony structures are unremarkable. No new sites of concern.

Overall impression: The tumor continues to shrink on Tagrisso. The mediastinal nodes are now subcentimeter and borderline by size criteria. This is an ongoing, deepening partial response compared to both the baseline and the first restaging CT. Very encouraging trajectory.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IIIB (T3N2M0) EGFR L858R-MUTANT NON-SMALL CELL LUNG CANCER, PULMONARY ADENOCARCINOMA -- ONGOING PARTIAL RESPONSE ON osi
Ms. Yuen is doing exceptionally well at 6 months on osi 80 mg daily. Her symptom burden has improved substantially -- dyspnea and cough largely resolved, performance status improved to ECOG 0. CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mrs. Yuen is a 63-year-old Chinese-American woman with Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, currently on first-line osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily, presenting urgently for evaluation following restaging CT and MRI brain that demonstrated systemic and intracranial disease progression. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mrs. Yuen was diagnosed approximately 14 months ago with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) non-small cell lung cancer, specifically an acinar-predominant pulmonary adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, measuring 4.8 cm at diagnosis with involvement of ipsilateral mediastinal lymph nodes at stations 4R and 7 confirmed by EBUS-guided biopsy. Molecular profiling at diagnosis demonstrated an EGFR exon 21 L858R sensitizing mutation (VAF 42% on tissue NGS), PD-L1 TPS 15% by 22C3 assay, negative ALK and ROS1, and a TMB of 6.2 mutations/megabase. Initial staging brain MRI was negative for intracranial metastases.

The multidisciplinary tumor board discussed her case extensively at diagnosis. While definitive concurrent chemoradiation followed by osimertinib maintenance per the LAURA trial data was the recommended standard approach, Mrs. Yuen declined chemoradiation given understandable concerns about exacerbating her underlying GOLD Stage I COPD. After thorough shared decision-making, she elected to proceed with upfront osimertinib monotherapy at 80 mg orally once daily.

Her initial response to osimertinib was gratifying. A restaging CT at 3 months showed a significant partial response -- the right upper lobe mass decreased from 4.8 cm to 2.6 cm, with corresponding shrinkage of the 4R and subcarinal lymphadenopathy. Her 9-month restaging CT showed further deepening of response, with the primary mass down to 1.8 cm and the nodal stations normalizing in size. At her 6-month clinical assessment, her ECOG performance status had improved to 0 and she reported resolution of dyspnea and cough.

Her course on osimertinib has been complicated by manageable toxicities: a grade 2 acneiform rash at approximately 2 months (managed with topical clindamycin, oral doxycycline, and a good moisturizing regimen, improving to grade 1 without dose reduction), grade 1 paronychia of bilateral first toes, and grade 1 diarrhea and dry eye syndrome appearing around month 4, both managed conservatively without dose interruption.

Now, at approximately 14 months from osimertinib initiation, she presents with alarming new symptoms. She describes progressive exertional dyspnea over the past 3-4 weeks -- she was previously able to walk several blocks comfortably but is now limited to less than one block before becoming short of breath. She notes a new intermittent pressure-like headache that is predominantly bifrontal, rated 3-5/10 in severity, present most days for the past 10 days. She does note mild fatigue over the same time period.

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast showed progressive disease in the chest: the right upper lobe mass has grown from its nadir of 1.8 cm to 3.2 cm, representing a 78% increase from nadir -- clearly unambiguous progression rather than pseudoprogression. The concurrent MRI brain with gadolinium, obtained given her new headache symptoms, revealed two new enhancing lesions: a 1.1 cm lesion in the right frontal lobe and a 0.8 cm lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. Both lesions are contrast-enhancing and are consistent with brain metastases in this clinical context. These were not present on the brain MRI obtained 14 months ago at initial staging.

ASSESSMENT:
Mrs. Yuen is a 63-year-old woman with Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma, presenting with clear radiographic progression on osimertinib (Tagrisso) after approximately 14 months of therapy. This represents acquired resistance to first-line osimertinib. The progression is multifocal: systemic (growth of right upper lobe primary, re-emergent mediastinal nodal disease, new right pleural effusion) and intracranial (two new brain metastases in the right frontal lobe and right cerebellar hemisphere). She is symptomatic from both domains -- dyspnea and cough from the pulmonary/pleural disease, and new headaches likely attributable to the intracranial lesions (although she fortunately has no focal neurologic deficits).

The development of acquired resistance to osimertinib is well recognized and has a median time to progression of approximately 10-18 months in this setting. The most common acquired resistance mechanisms include on-target EGFR C797S mutation (particularly in the context of prior T790M), bypass track mechanisms such as MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, BRAF activation, and histologic transformation (particularly to small cell lung cancer). Identification of the resistance mechanism is critical to selecting the optimal next-line therapy, as some mechanisms are directly druggable.

Although she was on third-generation osimertinib (which itself covers T790M), there are clinical scenarios in which T790M can persist or emerge as a subclonal resistance driver even in this setting; however, other bypass mechanisms are equally or more likely. A liquid biopsy for ctDNA analysis will be the first step, given its rapid turnaround and non-invasive nature, and will allow assessment for EGFR T790M, C797S, MET amplification, and other resistance drivers.

The brain metastases are a new and urgent development. Two small but clearly enhancing lesions are present. Given their relatively small size, her preserved neurologic function, and the absence of significant surrounding edema or mass effect, she is an excellent candidate for stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) rather than whole brain radiation, which we aim to avoid to preserve neurocognitive function.

---

PLAN:

1. Resistance Mechanism Workup -- Liquid Biopsy:
Ordering Guardant360 plasma ctDNA panel immediately. Blood draw obtained in clinic today. Key analytes of interest: EGFR T790M, EGFR exon 20 C797S, MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, BRAF V600E, and any novel emerging resistance mutations. Results expected within 7-10 business days. Tissue re-biopsy of the right upper lobe progression site may be considered if ctDNA is non-informative (i.e., low shedding), but ctDNA will be assessed first.

2. Intracranial Disease -- Radiation Oncology Referral:
Urgent referral placed to Dr. [Radiation Oncology Attending] for evaluation of the two brain metastases (right frontal lobe 1.1 cm, right cerebellar 0.8 cm) for stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS). SRS is preferred given the limited number of lesions, their small size, and the goal of preserving neurocognitive function. Brain MRI images transmitted electronically to radiation oncology for planning review. Neurosurgery will not be consulted at this time given the lack of neurologic deficit, small lesion size, and absence of accessible/surgically favorable anatomy (the cerebellar lesion is deepseated).

3. Systemic Therapy -- Interim Management:
Osimertinib 80 mg orally daily will be continued in the interim while resistance testing is pending and while SRS is being coordinated. Although osimertinib has clearly lost activity against the bulk of disease, continuing it during the evaluation and SRS coordination period is standard practice and may prevent rapid further systemic progression (so-called "drug holiday" effect after discontinuation can sometimes cause flare in EGFR-mutant NSCLC). The decision on next-line systemic therapy will be made after ctDNA results return and after SRS is completed.

4. Multidisciplinary Tumor Board:
Case to be re-presented at the weekly thoracic oncology tumor board. Discussion will center on next-line systemic therapy options following confirmation of the resistance mechanism. Options under consideration include: platinum-based doublet chemotherapy (carboplatin or cisplatin plus pemetrexed, given non-squamous histology, with or without bevacizumab), amivantamab plus lazertinib (MARIPOSA-2 schema), or clinical trial enrollment if eligible. Combination of amivantamab (EGFR/MET bispecific) plus lazertinib is of particular interest given its activity in osimertinib-resistant NSCLC demonstrated in MARIPOSA-2 and the possibility that the resistance mechanism may involve MET.

5. Symptom Management:
For headache: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg as needed (with food), not to exceed 3 days/week to avoid medication overuse headache. Patient instructed to call immediately if headaches worsen, become positional, awaken from sleep, are associated with nausea/vomiting, or if any neurologic symptoms emerge -- this would warrant urgent re-evaluation and consideration of corticosteroids for cerebral edema.
For dyspnea: Continue current antihypertensive and diabetes medications. No supplemental oxygen required at rest (SpO2 95% on RA). Activity modifications as needed. If dyspnea worsens significantly, chest imaging to assess for progression of pleural effusion with potential thoracentesis for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes.

6. Supportive Care:
Social work involvement to be continued. Mrs. Yuen and her daughter Linda were counseled at length today regarding the progression, the urgent need for brain evaluation and treatment, and the plan to find an effective next line of therapy. The conversation was difficult and emotional; we spent significant time addressing their concerns and questions. Patient expressed understanding and is committed to pursuing treatment. Psycho-oncology referral offered; patient will consider.

7. Follow-up:
Return in office 10-14 days (or sooner if symptoms worsen) to review ctDNA results, confirm SRS scheduling and status, and discuss tumor board recommendations for next-line systemic therapy. Patient to call our triage line if any new or worsening neurologic symptoms arise before the next appointment.

Total visit time: 55 minutes. More than 50% of visit time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

   Interpretation: This is the patient's known primary driver mutation, first detected in tissue at diagnosis (VAF 42% in FFPE core biopsy). Detection in plasma ctDNA at VAF 3.8% is consistent with residual circulating tumor DNA shedding from progressing disease. The relatively low VAF reflects the partial suppression of dominant EGFR L858R clone during Tagrisso treatment, with re-emergence corresponding to clinical progression.    Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 1.6%
   Classification: Pathogenic — Acquired resistance mutation
   Interpretation: EGFR T790M is detected at low VAF (1.6%) in this plasma specimen. In the context of 1st- and 2nd-generation EGFR TKI treatment (erlo, Iressa, afat, Vizimpro), T790M is the most common acquired resistance mechanism (~50-65% of cases), and in that setting would represent a targetable resistance mutation amenable to Tagrisso therapy. However, this patient was treated with upfront osi (3rd-generation TKI), in which T790M does NOT constitute a primary resistance mechanism. Detection of T790M in the setting of upfront osi is ATYPICAL and may reflect: (a) a pre-existing low-level subclonal T790M population present at baseline that was below the limit of detection of the original diagnostic PCR/NGS, now enriched under selective therapeutic pressure; (b) a coincidental co-occurring resistance biology; or (c) an emerging secondary EGFR pathway alteration. The clinical significance in the context of third-generation Tagrisso treatment requires further investigation. Notably, EGFR C797S — the canonical resistance mutation to osi — is NOT detected (see below).

SPECIMEN ADEQUACY: Adequate — circulating tumor DNA detected above established assay limits of detection.

KEY FINDINGS:
1. EGFR L858R confirmed in plasma ctDNA (VAF 3.8%) — consistent with active disease burden from the known primary EGFR driver mutation.
2. EGFR T790M DETECTED at low VAF (1.6%) — acquired resistance mutation detected. In the context of upfront (first-line) Tagrisso treatment, T790M positivity is atypical and does not conform to the expected osi resistance profile. This finding is most consistent with a low-level pre-existing subclonal T790M population that was not detectable at the time of original diagnosis (below the sensitivity threshold of initial cobas PCR/NGS testing) and has been enriched under selective pressure of osi therapy. Alternative possibility of a true de novo second-hit T790M emerging as an osi resistance mechanism cannot be fully excluded but is considered biologically less likely.
3. EGFR C797S NOT detected — excludes the most common canonical resistance mechanism to osi, suggesting additional or alternative bypass resistance pathways may be operative.
4. No MET amplification detected by cfDNA assay — however, sensitivity of plasma cfDNA-based copy number assessment is limited; tissue re-biopsy with comprehensive genomic profiling should be considered to fully exclude bypass track mechanisms (MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, oncogenic fusions) that are not optimally assessed by liquid biopsy.
5. TP53 R248W confirmed as co-mutation.

CLINICAL INTERPRETATION:
The detection of EGFR T790M in the context of upfront Tagrisso failure is uncommon. The treating oncologist should consider:
(a) The possibility that this T790M is not the primary driver of resistance, and that other bypass mechanisms not captured at adequate sensitivity by this cfDNA assay (e.g., MET amplification, histologic transformation to small cell) may be operationally responsible for clinical progression.
(b) Tissue re-biopsy of the accessible progressing lesion (right upper lobe mass) for comprehensive genomic profiling and histologic assessment (to exclude small cell or squamous transformation).
(c) Given the presence of T790M, novel combination approaches targeting EGFR L858R/T790M (e.g., EGFR-MET bispecific antibodies, amivantamab-lazertinib) and/or platinum-based chemotherapy may be considered in the context of clinical trial enrollment or standard of care, per multidisciplinary team decision.

This report is issued as a laboratory finding only. Therapeutic decisions remain the responsibility of the treating physician in consultation with the clinical team.

Ms. Yuen is a 63-year-old Chinese-American female with a known history of Stage IIIB pulmonary adenocarcinoma (right upper lobe; T3N2M0, AJCC 8th edition) harboring an EGFR exon 21 L858R sensitizing point mutation, initially identified on tissue-based NGS and confirmed by PCR at the time of diagnosis. She was treated with first-line osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily (initiated approximately 6 weeks following diagnosis), achieving a significant partial response by RECIST 1.1 criteria (primary mass reduction from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm; normalization of mediastinal lymphadenopathy) over the first 9 months of therapy.

Approximately 14 months after initiating osimertinib, restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and concurrent MRI brain with gadolinium demonstrated systemic and intracranial disease progression: the right upper lobe primary mass increased from 1.8 cm (nadir) to 3.2 cm, new right paratracheal lymphadenopathy was identified (1.4 cm), a new small right pleural effusion was noted, and two new enhancing brain lesions were identified (1.1 cm right frontal lobe; 0.8 cm right cerebellar hemisphere), consistent with brain metastases.

This liquid biopsy was ordered to characterize the molecular resistance landscape, specifically to evaluate for the presence of the EGFR T790M resistance mutation and other mechanisms of acquired resistance to third-generation EGFR TKI therapy.

Its detection in circulating tumor DNA at VAF 3.8% confirms continued shedding of EGFR-mutant tumor DNA into the plasma and is consistent with active disease. This mutation confers sensitivity to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). Of note, the relatively modest VAF in plasma likely reflects the dynamics of tumor shedding at the time of blood draw relative to total cfDNA input, rather than a low tumor burden signal per se, given the radiographically confirmed progressive disease.

2. EGFR p.T790M (c.2369C>T), Exon 20 | VAF: 1.6%
   Classification: ACQUIRED RESISTANCE MUTATION — Tier I (Strong clinical significance)
   Gene Function: Epidermal growth factor receptor tyrosine kinase
   Interpretation: The EGFR T790M substitution introduces a methionine at the gatekeeper position of the EGFR kinase domain (threonine 790), sterically impeding the binding of first- and second-generation EGFR TKIs (erlotinib, gefitinib, afatinib, dacomitinib) and conferring resistance. In the canonical clinical paradigm, T790M arises as an acquired resistance mutation in approximately 50-60% of patients who progress on first- or second-generation EGFR TKIs and is targetable by osimertinib (Tagrisso). However, in the present clinical context, this patient received osimertinib as her initial (first-line) EGFR-directed therapy, and its appearance here is atypical and clinically noteworthy.

   The detection of T790M at relatively low VAF (1.6%) in the context of upfront osimertinib progression is subject to two leading interpretations:

   (a) Pre-existing subclonal T790M population: A small T790M-mutant subclone may have been present below the detection limit of baseline tissue or liquid biopsy testing at the time of diagnosis. Under the selective pressure of osimertinib (which, although potent against T790M-positive cells, may exert differential clonal pressure across heterogeneous tumor subpopulations), this subclone may have expanded to detectable levels. This mechanism has been described in a subset of osimertinib-treated patients, particularly those who progress relatively early (within 12-18 months).

   (b) De novo T790M emergence / concurrent bypass mechanism: T790M may represent an independent resistance event arising in the context of molecular tumor evolution under osimertinib pressure. In this scenario, T790M co-exists with other bypass track resistance mechanisms (e.g., MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, BRAF alterations) that may be the primary drivers of clinical resistance, with T790M representing a secondary or parallel mechanism. Given the relatively low VAF (1.6%) compared to L858R (3.8%), the T790M-positive subclone does not appear to be the dominant circulating clone at this time point.

   Neither osimertinib continuation nor re-challenge with first-/second-generation EGFR TKIs would be expected to provide durable benefit in this setting. The detection of T790M in a patient progressing on osimertinib does not meet the standard clinical indication for osimertinib initiation (since the patient is already receiving this agent). This finding, in combination with the absence of other currently detected bypass mechanisms in this plasma specimen, will inform multi-disciplinary discussion regarding subsequent therapeutic options.

   IMPORTANT NOTE: Liquid biopsy has known false-negative limitations, particularly for copy number alterations (e.g., MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification) which may not be reliably detected in plasma-based assays. Tissue re-biopsy should be considered to comprehensively evaluate for bypass resistance mechanisms that may be underrepresented in ctDNA.

--- SECTION 2: CO-MUTATIONS AND SECONDARY ALTERATIONS ---

3. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T), Exon 7 | VAF: 5.2%
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC — Tier II
   Gene Function: Tumor suppressor; transcription factor regulating cell cycle arrest, apoptosis, and DNA damage response
   Interpretation: TP53 R248W is a well-characterized hotspot gain-of-function missense mutation at codon 248 (one of the most frequently mutated residues in human cancer), resulting in loss of wild-type tumor suppressive function and acquisition of oncogenic gain-of-function activities including promotion of invasion, resistance to apoptosis, and altered metabolic reprogramming. This mutation was previously identified in the patient's original tissue-based NGS at VAF 38% and is now detected in plasma at VAF 5.2%, consistent with continued clonal persistence. TP53 mutations are frequent co-mutations in EGFR-mutant NSCLC and have been associated with shorter progression-free survival on EGFR TKI therapy in some studies, though they are not independently targetable with currently approved agents. VAF ratio of TP53:EGFR L858R (5.2%:3.8% ≈ 1.4) is approximately consistent with co-occurrence in the same tumor clone, as expected.

4. ARID1A frameshift mutation (specific position: [VAF 0.9%])
   Classification: VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS) — Tier III
   Gene Function: SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex; tumor suppressor
   Interpretation: An ARID1A frameshift mutation is detected at very low VAF (0.9%), near the lower analytical sensitivity threshold of this assay. ARID1A was previously identified as a VUS (p.E1814*, VAF 18%) in the original tissue-based NGS. The low VAF in plasma may reflect a minor subclone or represent stochastic sampling variation in cfDNA. ARID1A loss-of-function has been described in a subset of lung adenocarcinomas and has been associated with increased sensitivity to immune checkpoint blockade in some tumor types, though clinical significance in this setting remains investigational.

--- SECTION 3: COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS ---

5. MET copy number: WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS (no amplification detected)
   Interpretation: MET amplification is one of the most clinically significant bypass resistance mechanisms to osimertinib, reported in approximately 15-26% of patients with acquired resistance to third-generation EGFR TKIs in tissue-based studies. The absence of detectable MET amplification by plasma ctDNA does NOT exclude MET amplification as a resistance mechanism, as copy number alterations are generally less sensitively detected in liquid biopsy compared to tissue NGS (particularly at lower levels of amplification). Tissue re-biopsy with dedicated FISH or tissue-based comprehensive genomic profiling is strongly recommended to definitively assess MET amplification status.

CLINICAL INTERPRETATION AND SUMMARY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

This Guardant360 plasma ctDNA analysis performed at disease progression on upfront osimertinib reveals the following key findings:

1. EGFR L858R confirmed (VAF 3.8%): The founding sensitizing driver mutation remains detectable in circulating tumor DNA, confirming continued EGFR-mutant tumor biology.

2. EGFR T790M detected (VAF 1.6%): An acquired resistance mutation is identified at low VAF. In the context of upfront (first-line) osimertinib therapy — a third-generation TKI designed to overcome T790M resistance — the emergence of T790M is clinically atypical and mechanistically complex. The low VAF relative to the founding L858R clone raises the question of whether this reflects a minor pre-existing subclone selected for under osimertinib pressure, rather than the dominant mechanism of clinical resistance. The absence of the EGFR C797S mutation rules out the triple-mutant resistance configuration.

3. No MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, or FGFR1 amplification detected in plasma: These are the most clinically relevant bypass track resistance mechanisms to osimertinib and are not detected in this plasma specimen. However, the sensitivity of liquid biopsy for copy number amplifications is significantly lower than for point mutations and SNVs, and a false-negative result for MET or ERBB2 amplification cannot be excluded. Given that MET amplification is one of the most common mechanisms of acquired osimertinib resistance and has direct therapeutic implications (including for EGFR/MET bispecific antibody strategies under investigation), tissue re-biopsy with comprehensive genomic profiling (e.g., FoundationOne CDx, tissue-based hybrid capture panel with robust CNV detection) is strongly recommended.

4. No EGFR C797S: The absence of C797S in the context of T790M means that allosteric EGFR inhibition strategies (e.g., osimertinib re-challenge in combination with first-generation TKIs in the cis-C797S/T790M configuration) would not be precluded on allele-configuration grounds; however, the clinical utility of such combinations in this scenario remains investigational.

5. TP53 R248W confirmed as a persistent co-mutation (VAF 5.2%): No new clinically actionable alterations identified.

Conclusion: Acquired EGFR T790M resistance mutation confirmed on liquid biopsy in the setting of disease progression on third-generation EGFR TKI (osimertinib). This finding is atypical in this context and likely reflects a pre-existing subclonal T790M population enriched under osimertinib selective pressure, potentially in concert with undetected bypass mechanisms. The treating team is advised to pursue tissue re-biopsy of an accessible progressing lesion for comprehensive genomic profiling to fully characterize the resistance landscape and guide selection among potential subsequent therapies, which may include amivantamab (EGFR/MET bispecific antibody) plus lazertinib, platinum-based chemotherapy doublet, or appropriately matched clinical trial enrollment.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Part A (CT-guided core needle biopsy, right upper lobe mass):
Histologic sections of the core needle biopsy demonstrate a cellular neoplasm infiltrating a desmoplastic stroma. The tumor is composed of atypical epithelial cells arranged predominantly in irregular glandular and acinar formations (acinar-predominant pattern per IASLC/ATS/ERS 2015 classification). Focally, neoplastic cells are arranged along intact alveolar septa in a lepidic growth pattern, comprising an estimated 15-20% of the tumor. The neoplastic cells exhibit moderate nuclear pleomorphism, with round to oval nuclei containing prominent nucleoli, finely granular chromatin, and moderate amounts of pale eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm. Mitotic figures are present, estimated at 4 per 10 high-power fields. No definitive vascular or lymphatic space invasion is identified within the biopsy. No necrosis is identified. The background lung parenchyma shows mild anthracotic pigment deposition and minimal emphysematous changes consistent with the patient's smoking history.

Part B (EBUS-FNA, right paratracheal lymph node, station 4R):
Smear preparations and cell block sections from the right paratracheal lymph node (station 4R) demonstrate numerous clusters and three-dimensional groups of malignant epithelial cells within a background of lymphoid elements and reactive lymphocytes. The malignant cells are morphologically identical to those identified in Part A, exhibiting acinar/glandular arrangements, moderate nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and moderate pale cytoplasm. Scattered single malignant cells and small cohesive clusters are present between sheets of small lymphocytes. The findings are consistent with metastatic adenocarcinoma, morphologically indistinguishable from the primary lung adenocarcinoma sampled in Part A. No extracapsular extension can be assessed on FNA material.

The histomorphologic and immunohistochemical findings confirm invasive pulmonary adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, acinar-predominant with focal lepidic features, consistent with a primary lung origin. The concurrent EBUS-FNA of the right paratracheal (station 4R) lymph node demonstrates nodal involvement by metastatic adenocarcinoma, consistent with N2 nodal disease in the context of the clinical and radiologic staging information provided.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Yuen's oncologic history is significant for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma diagnosed approximately 18 months ago. At the time of diagnosis, she was deemed unresectable due to multi-station N2 nodal involvement at stations 4R and 7. She declined concurrent chemoradiation given her underlying COPD (GOLD Stage I) and, after extensive multidisciplinary discussion, elected to proceed with upfront Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily. She had an excellent initial response, with her primary right upper lobe mass decreasing from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm at approximately 9 months. Unfortunately, restaging imaging at approximately 14 months showed unequivocal progression: the primary mass grew back to 3.2 cm, the right paratracheal nodes re-enlarged, a new small right pleural effusion appeared, and brain MRI revealed two new enhancing lesions in the right frontal lobe (1.1 cm) and right cerebellar hemisphere (0.8 cm), consistent with intracranial metastases.

Liquid biopsy at that time (Guardant360) and subsequent tissue NGS (FoundationOne CDx) confirmed acquired resistance, with EGFR T790M detected alongside the original L858R. Notably, a bypass track resistance mechanism was also identified on plasma ctDNA and confirmed on repeat tissue biopsy -- MET amplification (copy number 6.8 on subsequent re-biopsy) was later identified as the dominant resistance driver.

She was referred to radiation oncology and underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) with a frameless Varian Edge system to both brain metastases (right frontal: 20 Gy x 1 fraction; right cerebellar: 22 Gy x 1 fraction) approximately 15 months after her original diagnosis. She tolerated SRS well, with only transient grade 1 fatigue and headache managed with a brief course of dexamethasone and acetaminophen.

Following SRS, osi was discontinued and she was started on second-line systemic chemotherapy with Paraplatin (AUC 5) plus Alimta (500 mg/m2) plus Avastin (15 mg/kg), all administered intravenously on day 1 of a 21-day cycle. Folic acid 400 mcg orally daily and vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks were initiated per standard Alimta supplementation protocol.

She has now completed 3 cycles of this regimen. Her most recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain demonstrated a favorable response: the right upper lobe primary mass has decreased from 3.2 cm at progression to 2.4 cm -- a roughly 25% reduction from the progression nadir. The right paratracheal lymphadenopathy has also decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm, and the right pleural effusion that was present at progression has resolved completely. Brain MRI is very encouraging: the right frontal SRS-treated lesion has shrunk from 1.1 cm to 0.6 cm, and the cerebellar lesion from 0.8 cm to 0.4 cm, both consistent with a brisk post-SRS treatment response, though radiation necrosis cannot be entirely excluded at this point.

Today she reports that her energy has noticeably improved compared to the time of disease progression. She notes significantly reduced shortness of breath -- she is now able to perform her activities of daily living and walk around her neighborhood without stopping, which she was unable to do during the progression period. Headaches, which were a concerning symptom at the time brain metastases were discovered, have largely resolved. She is sleeping better and her appetite has returned. She acknowledges persistent but manageable nausea, particularly in the 24-48 hours following infusion, and mild fatigue that improves by day 5-6 of each cycle. She denies any fever, chills, hemoptysis, chest pain, or new neurologic symptoms including no new focal weakness, vision changes, or difficulties with coordination or balance. She has not had any bleeding episodes. Bowel habits have been somewhat loose at times but nothing that meets criteria for grade 2 diarrhea. Peripheral neuropathy has not been a significant complaint through the first three cycles.

Her daughter, who lives nearby and accompanies her to most appointments, reports that her mother's mood and engagement have improved substantially since the chemotherapy appeared to be working. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IIIB EGFR L858R-MUTANT PULMONARY ADENOCARCINOMA, NOW WITH INTRACRANIAL METASTASES, STATUS POST SRS -- PARTIAL RESPONSE TO SECOND-LINE carbo/Alimta/Avastin

Ms. Yuen has done well with her second-line chemotherapy regimen and we are seeing a meaningful response in her chest and continued regression of her SRS-treated brain lesions. She has completed 3 of a planned 4-6 cycles of the induction chemotherapy phase. Given the response we are seeing and her overall good tolerance, the plan is to proceed with cycle 4 of carbo/Alimta/Avastin.

After completing induction, the current plan -- which is consistent with standard of care for non-squamous NSCLC with platinum-based chemotherapy induction -- is to transition to maintenance peme plus bev. We will continue this until disease progression or prohibitive toxicity. There are good data supporting this approach for non-squamous NSCLC including from the PARAMOUNT and ECOG 4599/PointBreak-type data that have informed this strategy. Her non-squamous (adenocarcinoma) histology and absence of bev-related hypertension or proteinuria make her an appropriate candidate.

Will monitor for any new neurologic symptoms and act promptly if they develop.

4. DISCUSSION OF FUTURE THERAPY OPTIONS AND CLINICAL TRIAL OPPORTUNITIES

Given the nature of Ms. Yuen's disease -- EGFR L858R-mutant NSCLC with acquired resistance mechanisms including EGFR T790M and now confirmed MET amplification (on prior tissue NGS) -- she faces a challenging resistance landscape beyond the current chemotherapy regimen. We had an important conversation today with both Ms. Yuen and her daughter about what comes next.

I introduced the concept of clinical trial options that are becoming available for patients with exactly her molecular profile -- specifically, those who have progressed on an EGFR TKI and are now on or will have completed platinum-based chemotherapy. I discussed the rationale for dual EGFR/MET targeting given her MET amplification, which is a known bypass mechanism in EGFR-mutant NSCLC after Tagrisso. I also discussed the role of immunotherapy in this population, noting that her PD-L1 TPS was 28% on the most recent biopsy.

Our research coordinator was introduced to Ms. Yuen and her daughter today and will provide detailed information about a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody in combination with an anti-PD-L1 agent for patients with EGFR-mutant NSCLC after TKI and chemotherapy exposure. The patient and her daughter expressed genuine enthusiasm and asked several thoughtful questions about what participation would involve, including frequency of visits and potential side effects. Screening procedures were briefly outlined; formal eligibility screening will occur if and when she completes induction chemotherapy and moves toward trial enrollment. The research coordinator will call within the week to schedule a formal pre-screening visit.

I was transparent with Ms. Yuen that despite the good response she is having right now, there is a meaningful probability of eventual progression on the current regimen, and having a well-defined next step is important. She appreciated the forthright conversation.

HbA1c 7.1% -- slightly above her previous baseline of 6.9%, likely partially related to steroid use on infusion days. Will discuss with her primary care physician regarding monitoring. Metformin dose maintained. Blood pressure well-controlled today at 128/76 on lisinopril. No adjustments needed.

7. RETURN VISIT

Ms. Yuen will return in 3 weeks for cycle 4 of Paraplatin/pem/Avastin (day 1). Labs (CBC, CMP, urine protein:creatinine) to be drawn 24-48 hours prior to infusion per standard protocol. She understands to call the oncology triage line immediately for fever > 38.3°C, significant bleeding, severe shortness of breath, new neurologic symptoms, or other concerning changes.

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Margaret Yuen is a 64-year-old Chinese-American woman with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0 at diagnosis, now with distant metastatic disease reflecting biologic progression) pulmonary adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, presenting for her cycle 1 week 4 on-trial assessment. She is enrolled on a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody in combination with a novel anti-PD-L1 agent. She presents today for scheduled on-study safety assessment, toxicity review, and blood work. She is accompanied by her daughter.

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**
Ms. Yuen has a well-established history of EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma diagnosed initially at age 62 as Stage IIIB disease (4.8 cm right upper lobe primary, multi-station N2 nodal involvement at stations 4R and 7 on PET-CT and EBUS). She is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit approximately 12 years prior to diagnosis). Background comorbidities include hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus (well-controlled, HbA1c historically in the 6.8-7.1% range), and mild COPD (GOLD Stage I).

Her oncologic course has been eventful. She initially declined concurrent chemoradiation and was treated with upfront osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as first-line therapy for her unresectable Stage IIIB EGFR-mutant disease, achieving a substantial partial response -- her primary tumor shrank from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm over approximately 9 months, and mediastinal nodes normalized. Unfortunately, at approximately 14 months on osimertinib, she developed systemic and intracranial progression: the RUL mass enlarged to 3.2 cm, right paratracheal nodes re-appeared, a small right pleural effusion developed, and brain MRI revealed two new enhancing brain metastases (right frontal 1.1 cm, right cerebellar hemisphere 0.8 cm). Liquid biopsy and subsequent tissue re-biopsy both confirmed acquired EGFR T790M resistance, and, critically, Foundation One CDx performed on the repeat tissue biopsy prior to trial enrollment demonstrated high-level MET amplification (copy number 6.8) as a co-existing bypass resistance mechanism -- a finding directly relevant to her enrollment on this EGFR/MET bispecific trial.

She underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to both brain metastases (right frontal 20 Gy/1 fraction, right cerebellar 22 Gy/1 fraction), tolerated this well, and then proceeded to second-line carboplatin (AUC 5) / pemetrexed (500 mg/m2) / bevacizumab (15 mg/kg) q21 days, completing 4 cycles with a partial response (RUL mass down to 2.4 cm), followed by transition to maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab. During maintenance she developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy from prior carboplatin, managed with duloxetine, and mild bevacizumab-related hypertension managed with addition of amlodipine. At approximately 11 months into second-line therapy (6 months of maintenance), she progressed again: the RUL mass grew to 3.8 cm, new bilateral pulmonary nodules appeared (RLL 0.8 cm, LLL 0.6 cm), and the previously SRS-treated right frontal lesion enlarged to 1.4 cm with surrounding edema -- MRI perfusion and MR spectroscopy favored tumor recurrence over radiation necrosis (elevated rCBV, elevated choline:NAA ratio). She received repeat SRS to the right frontal recurrence (18 Gy/1 fraction, reduced dose given prior irradiation) and was started on a short course of dexamethasone for peritumoral edema, which has now been fully tapered.

Her tumor re-biopsy confirmed: EGFR L858R (VAF 48%), EGFR T790M (VAF 22%, elevated compared to prior ctDNA), MET amplification (CN 6.8), RB1 nonsense mutation newly detected (p.Q670*, VAF 18%), TP53 R248W (VAF 52%), CDK4 and CCNE1 low-level amplifications, and PD-L1 TPS 28% (increased from 15% at diagnosis). No small cell transformation on histology. On the basis of this profile -- specifically the EGFR L858R/T790M co-mutation with MET amplification -- she met eligibility criteria and was enrolled on trial. She received cycle 1, day 1 dosing approximately 4 weeks ago, following informed consent discussion with herself and her daughter.

Today she returns for her scheduled C1W4 visit. Overall, she reports tolerating treatment reasonably well. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Yuen's oncologic history is extensive and is summarized as follows for context:

She was initially diagnosed approximately 2 years ago with Stage IIIB right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma (T3N2M0 by AJCC 8th edition) on the basis of a 4.8 cm spiculated right upper lobe mass with PET-avid ipsilateral mediastinal nodal involvement at stations 4R and 7. Tissue biopsy confirmed invasive pulmonary adenocarcinoma, acinar-predominant, TTF-1 positive. Molecular profiling at diagnosis revealed an EGFR exon 21 L858R sensitizing mutation (VAF 42%), TP53 R248W co-mutation, STK11/KEAP1 wild-type, no MET amplification, PD-L1 TPS 15%, and low TMB (6.2 mut/Mb) by a 500-gene NGS panel. ALK, ROS1, RET, and NTRK fusions were all negative.

At the time of diagnosis, multidisciplinary tumor board review determined the tumor to be unresectable given multi-station N2 involvement. Concurrent chemoradiation was discussed but declined by the patient due to concern about compounding toxicity in the setting of her underlying GOLD Stage I COPD. After extensive shared decision-making, upfront osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily was initiated as definitive-intent first-line therapy. She achieved a marked partial response — the primary mass shrank from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm at 9 months, and mediastinal lymph nodes normalized in size. She tolerated osimertinib reasonably well, with grade 2 acneiform rash managed with topical clindamycin and oral doxycycline (no dose reduction required), grade 1 paronychia of bilateral first toes, grade 1 diarrhea, and grade 1 dry eye syndrome. At her best response timepoint, her ECOG performance status had improved to 0.

At approximately 14 months on osimertinib, restaging CT chest and MRI brain demonstrated systemic and intracranial progression: the right upper lobe primary mass rebounded to 3.2 cm, new right paratracheal lymphadenopathy appeared, a small right pleural effusion emerged, and two new enhancing brain lesions were identified — a 1.1 cm right frontal lobe lesion and a 0.8 cm right cerebellar lesion. Liquid biopsy (Guardant360 ctDNA panel) revealed acquired EGFR T790M (VAF 1.6%) alongside persistent EGFR L858R and TP53 R248W; no C797S, MET amplification, or ERBB2 amplification was detected in plasma at that time.

She then underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to both brain metastases using the Varian Edge system (right frontal: 20 Gy x 1; right cerebellar: 22 Gy x 1) with good intracranial response on subsequent MRI. She was transitioned to second-line platinum-based chemotherapy with carboplatin (AUC 5) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2) plus bevacizumab (15 mg/kg) every 3 weeks. She completed 4 cycles of induction with confirmed partial systemic response (primary mass 2.4 cm), and then transitioned to maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab. On maintenance, she developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy in bilateral feet (attributed to prior carboplatin; managed with duloxetine 60 mg daily) and grade 1 bevacizumab-related hypertension (amlodipine 5 mg added).

At approximately 6 months of maintenance (roughly 11 months after initiating second-line therapy), repeat restaging imaging documented second systemic progression: the right upper lobe mass enlarged from 2.2 cm to 3.8 cm, new bilateral pulmonary nodules appeared (right lower lobe 0.8 cm, left lower lobe 0.6 cm), and the previously SRS-treated right frontal lesion increased from 0.6 cm to 1.4 cm with surrounding edema. MRI perfusion and spectroscopy were performed to differentiate radiation necrosis from tumor recurrence; elevated rCBV and a high choline:NAA ratio favored true tumor recurrence over necrosis. Maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab was discontinued.

Repeat SRS was delivered to the right frontal recurrent lesion (18 Gy x 1, dose-reduced given prior irradiation of the same volume). The cerebellar lesion remained resolved and did not require retreatment. She was started on a short course of dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for peritumoral edema.

Prior to trial enrollment, repeat tissue biopsy of the right upper lobe mass was performed for re-profiling. CLINICAL INDICATION: 64-year-old female with known metastatic non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma (EGFR L858R-mutant). History of stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to right frontal lobe metastasis (20 Gy in 1 fraction) and right cerebellar hemisphere metastasis (22 Gy in 1 fraction) approximately 11-12 months prior.
