Dedicated advanced MRI sequences obtained to distinguish radiation necrosis from viable tumor recurrence prior to planned management decisions.

COMPARISON: Prior MRI brain with gadolinium (approximately 4 weeks prior, at which time the right frontal lesion measured 1.4 cm with perilesional T2/FLAIR signal). Original post-SRS follow-up MRI (approximately 10 weeks after SRS, showing right frontal lesion at 0.6 cm with decrease from the pre-treatment size of 1.1 cm).

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar multisequence MRI of the brain performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner. Sequences include: sagittal T1, axial T2, axial FLAIR, axial DWI (b=0, 1000), axial susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), axial T1 pre-contrast, dynamic susceptibility contrast perfusion imaging using gadolinium bolus tracking, post-contrast axial and coronal T1-weighted images (MPRAGE), and multivoxel 2D chemical shift imaging (2D-CSI) MR spectroscopy centered on the right frontal lesion and a symmetric contralateral reference region. Intravenous gadolinium contrast administered without complication.

FINDINGS:

Right Frontal Lobe (Primary Site of Interest):
There is an irregularly marginated area of predominantly ring-like and nodular enhancement in the right frontal lobe (white matter, posterior to the precentral gyrus), currently measuring approximately 1.4 x 1.2 cm in axial dimensions (compared to 0.6 x 0.5 cm on the most recent prior MRI approximately 4 weeks ago, and compared to 1.1 x 0.9 cm at initial presentation prior to SRS). There is surrounding T2/FLAIR hyperintensity extending over a roughly 3.5 cm zone, considerably more prominent than on the prior study, consistent with worsening vasogenic edema and/or treatment effect involving the adjacent white matter. Mild local mass effect on the right frontal horn of the lateral ventricle is noted; no significant midline shift. No restricted diffusion within the lesion to suggest acute ischemic change. SWI demonstrates a focus of susceptibility artifact at the periphery of the lesion, likely reflecting hemosiderin or prior blood products related to treatment. There is no new hemorrhage.

DSC Perfusion Imaging -- Right Frontal Lesion:
Relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) mapping demonstrates focally elevated rCBV within the enhancing nodular component of the right frontal lesion, with an rCBV ratio of approximately 2.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter (threshold for concern for viable tumor generally accepted at rCBV ratio >2.0-2.5 at this institution). The rCBV elevation is most pronounced in the posterior and inferior portion of the ring-enhancement, corresponding to the region of most prominent nodular enhancement on post-contrast T1 images. A small area of relatively lower rCBV is also present within the central portion of the lesion, which may reflect a necrotic core. Overall, the perfusion pattern is not typical of pure radiation necrosis (which classically demonstrates reduced rCBV) and is more consistent with a viable, hypervascular tumor component.

MR Spectroscopy -- Right Frontal Lesion:
Multivoxel 2D-CSI MR spectroscopy was performed with voxel placement encompassing the enhancing lesion and surrounding perilesional tissue, with a reference voxel placed in the contralateral left frontal white matter. Within the voxel most closely overlapping the enhancing nodular component:
- Choline (Cho) peak is markedly elevated
- N-acetylaspartate (NAA) peak is markedly reduced
- Choline:NAA (Cho:NAA) ratio is elevated at approximately 2.6 (normal <1.0; ratio >2.0 highly suspicious for viable neoplasm at this institution)
- Creatine (Cr) peak is moderately reduced
- A small lipid/lactate peak is present, which can be seen in both necrosis and high-grade tumor
- The Cho:Cr ratio within the lesion is elevated compared to the contralateral reference voxel
In the perilesional voxels, there is a moderate reduction in NAA with less prominent choline elevation, consistent with treatment effect and edema in the adjacent white matter.
The spectroscopic profile, taken in conjunction with the perfusion findings, is most consistent with viable tumor recurrence rather than pure radiation necrosis. A mixed picture (i.e., tumor admixed with some treatment-related change) cannot be entirely excluded.

Right Cerebellar Hemisphere (Previously Treated Lesion):
The previously identified and SRS-treated right cerebellar hemisphere metastasis is no longer visualized as a discrete enhancing lesion. There is a small focal area of T2 signal change measuring approximately 0.3 cm in greatest dimension without enhancement, likely representing residual gliotic change or radiation-related change at the prior treatment site. No recurrence identified.

Remainder of the Brain:
No new enhancing lesions are identified on the current study. No additional parenchymal metastases. No leptomeningeal enhancement to suggest carcinomatous meningitis. The cerebral sulci and gyral pattern appear grossly within normal limits for patient age without evidence of global leptomeningeal disease. Ventricles are otherwise normal in caliber. No hydrocephalus. Brainstem and posterior fossa structures appear intact outside the previously described cerebellar treatment site change. Sella and skull base appear unremarkable. The major dural venous sinuses appear patent on post-contrast imaging.

Calvarial Structures and Orbits:
No aggressive osseous lesion identified within the calvarium. Orbits are grossly intact.

IMPRESSION:

1. Enlarging right frontal enhancing lesion (1.4 x 1.2 cm, increased from 0.6 x 0.5 cm on prior study), now accompanied by substantially increased surrounding vasogenic edema, at the site of prior stereotactic radiosurgery. The combination of elevated rCBV on DSC perfusion imaging (rCBV ratio approximately 2.8) and a markedly elevated choline:NAA ratio on MR spectroscopy (approximately 2.6) is most in keeping with viable tumor recurrence/regrowth rather than radiation necrosis alone. A mixed tumor/necrosis picture cannot be definitively excluded without tissue confirmation.

2. Previously treated right cerebellar hemisphere lesion: no residual enhancing disease; small focal T2 signal abnormality at prior SRS site consistent with treatment sequela.

3. No new intracranial metastases identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement.

18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG), 10.2 mCi administered intravenously. Uptake period: 63 minutes. Blood glucose at time of injection: 108 mg/dL. Attenuation-corrected PET images were reconstructed and co-registered with the contrast-enhanced CT. Images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes on dedicated PET-CT workstation. Maximum intensity projections (MIP) reviewed in conjunction with cross-sectional imaging.

Primary Mass -- Right Upper Lobe:
There is a dominant, intensely hypermetabolic mass in the posterior right upper lobe, centered near the origin of the posterior segmental bronchus of the right upper lobe. No post-obstructive atelectasis or consolidation is identified distal to the lesion. Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodule or mass identified. No abnormal FDG uptake in either adrenal gland. The kidneys are symmetric in size and demonstrate normal cortical thickness and enhancement. The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are unremarkable without focal wall thickening, mass, or hypermetabolic mucosal lesion. Physiologic FDG uptake in the bowel wall is noted. No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.

Peritoneum and Retroperitoneum:
No ascites. No peritoneal or mesenteric nodularity suspicious for carcinomatosis. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy with abnormal FDG uptake. No para-aortic, aortocaval, or iliac chain adenopathy. The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber.

Pelvis:
No pelvic mass or lymphadenopathy. Uterus and ovaries not well-evaluated given physiologic activity, however no dominant pelvic lesion identified. Bladder wall is unremarkable. Physiologic urinary tracer excretion in the bladder.

SKELETAL SURVEY:
Axial and appendicular skeleton reviewed on both CT and PET images. No lytic, blastic, or sclerotic lesions suspicious for osseous metastases. No abnormal focal or diffuse skeletal FDG uptake above physiologic marrow background. No pathologic fracture. Degenerative changes noted in the lower thoracic and lumbar spine (mild multilevel facet arthrosis, disc space narrowing at L4-L5), which are not hypermetabolic and consistent with age-appropriate degenerative disease. No rib lesions.

SOFT TISSUES:
No abnormal soft tissue mass or subcutaneous nodularity with hypermetabolic activity. No axillary, epitrochlear, or inguinal adenopathy with FDG uptake.

BRAIN NOTE:
The brain was not included in the PET-CT acquisition field of view, consistent with protocol. A dedicated contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain has been separately ordered for complete intracranial staging.

IMPRESSION:

1. INTENSELY HYPERMETABOLIC RIGHT UPPER LOBE MASS: A dominant spiculated mass measuring 4.8 cm in the posterior right upper lobe demonstrates intense FDG avidity (SUVmax 14.2), morphologically and metabolically highly suspicious for primary malignancy. The imaging appearance is most consistent with primary pulmonary carcinoma. Direct apposition of the right upper lobe bronchus is noted without definitive endobronchial extension on this study.

2. HYPERMETABOLIC RIGHT MEDIASTINAL LYMPHADENOPATHY INVOLVING STATIONS 4R AND 7: Enlarged and markedly FDG-avid right paratracheal (station 4R, SUVmax 8.7) and subcarinal (station 7, SUVmax 6.4) lymph nodes are consistent with ipsilateral mediastinal nodal involvement. These findings are consistent with N2 nodal disease in the context of a right-sided primary lung tumor.

3. INDETERMINATE RIGHT HILAR NODAL ACTIVITY (STATION 10R): Mildly enlarged right hilar lymph nodes with borderline FDG uptake (SUVmax 2.8) are indeterminate; correlation with endobronchial ultrasound-guided sampling is recommended for definitive characterization.

4. NO EVIDENCE OF DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE WITHIN THE IMAGING FIELD OF VIEW: No hypermetabolic lesions in the liver, adrenal glands, bones, soft tissues, or abdominal/pelvic lymph node chains to suggest distant spread. The adrenal glands are specifically normal and non-hypermetabolic.

5. NO PLEURAL EFFUSION OR PLEURAL NODULARITY.

6. BACKGROUND COPD CHANGES: Mild centrilobular emphysema, upper lobe predominant, without bullous change.

7. BRAIN NOT IMAGED ON THIS STUDY: Dedicated brain MRI pending for intracranial staging.

Overall, the findings are most consistent with a locally advanced right upper lobe primary lung malignancy with ipsilateral mediastinal nodal involvement (stations 4R and 7), without identifiable distant metastatic disease on this examination. The combined morphologic and metabolic profile strongly favors a T3N2M0 staging configuration pending intracranial evaluation. Tissue sampling and correlation with bronchoscopic/EBUS assessment of mediastinal nodal stations is recommended to confirm histology and complete nodal staging.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Hematoxylin and eosin-stained sections from Specimens A and B are examined. The biopsy cores show an infiltrating neoplasm composed predominantly of gland-forming epithelial cells arranged in an acinar pattern. The neoplastic glands are irregular in size and shape, lined by cuboidal to columnar cells with moderate to markedly enlarged nuclei, vesicular chromatin, and often conspicuous central nucleoli. The cytoplasm is moderate in amount, pale eosinophilic, and focally mucinous. Occasional nuclear pseudoinclusions are noted, a characteristic feature of pulmonary adenocarcinoma. The tumor glands are embedded within a desmoplastic fibrous stroma with a chronic inflammatory infiltrate. A minor component of solid growth pattern is noted (estimated <10%), not sufficient to alter the acinar-predominant classification per IASLC/ATS/ERS 2021 criteria. Focal central coagulative necrosis is present within some glandular lumina.

No organoid nesting, trabecular architecture, nuclear molding, granular "salt-and-pepper" chromatin, or Indian file growth pattern is identified to suggest neuroendocrine differentiation. The neoplastic cells do not demonstrate intercellular bridges or overt keratinization. No spindle cell component is identified. No features of sarcomatoid or pleomorphic carcinoma are seen.

The background non-neoplastic lung tissue shows mild chronic inflammatory changes and focal organizing pneumonia pattern, likely related to prior radiation therapy (the patient received SRS to intracranial sites; however, the right upper lobe was not directly irradiated) or prior chemotherapy effect. Reactive type II pneumocyte hyperplasia is noted at the biopsy edges.

Mitotic figures: approximately 6-8 per 10 high-power fields, consistent with increased proliferative activity as corroborated by Ki-67 index (~35-40%).

Touch preparations (Specimen C): The air-dried and ethanol-fixed preparations show loosely cohesive clusters and single malignant epithelial cells. The cells have eccentric nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate pale cytoplasm, consistent with adenocarcinoma. No necrotic background. No neuroendocrine features.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
COMMENT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This repeat biopsy of the right upper lobe mass in this patient with a known EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma demonstrates persistent adenocarcinoma of acinar-predominant type without histologic transformation. In the context of EGFR TKI resistance, phenotypic transformation to small cell carcinoma (SCLC) or squamous cell carcinoma represents a well-documented resistance mechanism. This specimen does not demonstrate such transformation, which is consistent with the clinical decision to pursue EGFR/MET-targeted investigational therapy.

Notably, PD-L1 TPS has increased to 28% from the initial 15% measured at diagnosis. This finding may reflect clonal evolution of the tumor immune microenvironment, shifts in the dominant tumor clone under selective treatment pressure, or inter-biopsy sampling heterogeneity. The increased PD-L1 expression could be of potential relevance if checkpoint inhibitor therapy is considered as part of the investigational regimen.

The Ki-67 proliferative index of approximately 35-40% is higher than typically reported for low-grade adenocarcinoma and may reflect increasing tumor aggressiveness in the setting of multi-line therapy and emerging resistance mechanisms.

Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) and MET FISH results are pending at time of sign-out. Results will be addended to this report upon availability.

GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY

CLINICALLY RELEVANT ALTERATIONS (TIER I / TIER II -- ACTIONABLE OR LIKELY ACTIONABLE)

1. EGFR p.L858R (Exon 21, missense substitution)
   - Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48%
   - Classification: Activating / Sensitizing mutation (Tier I -- established clinical significance)
   - Interpretation: Classic EGFR sensitizing mutation (exon 21 L858R point mutation). This alteration is the founding oncogenic driver identified at initial diagnosis in this patient. The high VAF (48%) in the current tissue specimen, increased substantially from the ctDNA VAF of 3.8% detected on prior liquid biopsy, reflects enrichment of the EGFR-mutant clone in the setting of acquired therapeutic resistance. This mutation confers sensitivity to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) including Tagrisso (Tagrisso), Tarceva, Iressa, and afat. The patient has already received first-line Tagrisso with a durable partial response followed by acquired resistance; L858R confirmation is required for eligibility on the planned clinical trial.

2. EGFR p.T790M (Exon 20, missense substitution, c.2369C>T)
   - Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22%
   - Classification: Acquired resistance mutation (Tier I -- established clinical significance in the context of prior EGFR TKI therapy)
   - Interpretation: The gatekeeper T790M substitution confers resistance to first-generation (Tarceva, gefi) and second-generation (Gilotrif, daco) EGFR TKIs by steric hindrance of inhibitor binding and restoration of ATP affinity. In this patient, T790M was first detected at low VAF (1.6%) on prior plasma ctDNA analysis at the time of first progression on Tagrisso. In the current tissue biopsy, T790M VAF has risen to 22%, co-occurring with the primary L858R sensitizing mutation. The emergence of T790M in the context of upfront third-generation Tagrisso (Tagrisso) therapy is biologically atypical, as osi retains potent activity against T790M-bearing tumors; it is postulated that the T790M clone identified here represents a pre-existing subclonal population that was selectively enriched under Tagrisso selective pressure, and/or that additional bypass resistance mechanisms (see MET amplification, below) have emerged concurrently. EGFR C797S, which would confer tertiary resistance to osi in the context of T790M, was NOT detected in this specimen.
   NOTE: The EGFR L858R and T790M mutations are confirmed to co-occur in cis based on read-level analysis consistent with a single resistant clonal population.

3. MET Amplification (Copy Number: 6.8; High-Level Amplification)
   - Classification: Acquired resistance mechanism / Actionable bypass track alteration (Tier I -- established clinical significance)
   - Interpretation: High-level MET gene amplification (copy number 6.8 by sequencing-based copy number analysis) is detected in this specimen. MET amplification is one of the most clinically significant and well-characterized bypass resistance mechanisms to EGFR TKI therapy in EGFR-mutant NSCLC, identified in approximately 5--20% of patients progressing on Tagrisso. Amplification of MET leads to activation of downstream RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT survival signaling independent of EGFR inhibition. MET amplification was NOT detected on prior plasma ctDNA analysis, suggesting either tissue-plasma discordance (lower ctDNA shedding of the MET-amplified subclone at that time) or subsequent clonal evolution and outgrowth. The detection of concurrent EGFR L858R/T790M mutations with high-level MET amplification defines a patient with multiple, potentially co-occurring, complementary resistance mechanisms. This combination of alterations is directly relevant to the planned clinical trial targeting both EGFR and MET via an investigational bispecific antibody approach. MET amplification in this degree (CN >6) is associated with responsiveness to MET-targeted strategies (e.g., tepotinib, capmatinib, savolitinib) and supports the bispecific EGFR/MET therapeutic hypothesis.

CO-MUTATIONS AND ADDITIONAL ALTERATIONS OF POTENTIAL CLINICAL RELEVANCE

4. TP53 p.R248W (Exon 7, missense substitution)
   - VAF: 52%
   - Classification: Likely Pathogenic / Co-mutation (Tier II)
   - Interpretation: TP53 R248W is a hotspot gain-of-function mutation at codon 248, one of the most frequently mutated residues in human cancer. This alteration has been present consistently across all prior molecular testing (initial tissue NGS VAF 38%; ctDNA VAF 5.2%; now tissue VAF 52%), confirming its status as an early truncal event in this tumor's evolutionary history. TP53 co-mutation in EGFR-mutant NSCLC is associated with a modestly worse prognosis and, in some data sets, shorter duration of benefit from EGFR TKI therapy. The increasing VAF (38% --> 52% from initial diagnosis to second progression) likely reflects increasing clonal dominance in the context of therapeutic selection.

5. RB1 p.Q670* (Exon 20, nonsense mutation; c.2008C>T)
   - VAF: 18%
   - Classification: Likely Pathogenic (Tier II -- emerging clinical relevance)
   - Interpretation: Newly detected in this specimen (not identified on initial tissue NGS or prior ctDNA analysis). RB1 loss-of-function mutations in EGFR-mutant NSCLC have been associated with accelerated resistance to EGFR TKI therapy, phenotypic plasticity, and an increased risk of histologic transformation (including small cell transformation, which was not identified on the current pathology specimen but warrants continued surveillance). RB1 p.Q670* introduces a premature stop codon, likely resulting in a truncated, non-functional protein and/or nonsense-mediated decay. This new RB1 alteration may be contributing to the evolving resistance biology in this tumor. The relatively modest VAF (18%) suggests either a subclonal distribution or partial allelic loss. Correlation with RB1 immunohistochemistry may be informative.

6. ARID1A p.E1814* (Exon 20, nonsense mutation)
   - VAF: 24%
   - Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (Tier III) -- VAF increased from 18% (initial tissue) to 24% (current tissue)
   - Interpretation: ARID1A encodes a subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex. Loss-of-function mutations in ARID1A have been observed across multiple tumor types and may influence sensitivity to certain therapeutic agents (e.g., HDAC inhibitors, EZH2 inhibitors, immune checkpoint inhibitors via mismatch repair interactions). In the context of this patient's tumor, ARID1A E1814* was initially detected as a VUS with modest VAF; its clonal expansion over time (18% --> 24%) suggests it is not a benign variant. Clinical significance in EGFR-mutant NSCLC at this site remains incompletely defined. No established actionable therapy is available for this alteration in isolation.

GENOMIC INTERPRETATION AND SUMMARY

This comprehensive genomic profiling report (FoundationOne CDx) was performed on a repeat CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right upper lobe primary tumor obtained at the time of second systemic disease progression, for the purpose of resistance mechanism characterization and clinical trial eligibility confirmation.

Key findings:

1. EGFR L858R (VAF 48%) is confirmed as the founding oncogenic driver, present at high clonal frequency. This mutation is required for enrollment on the planned EGFR/MET bispecific antibody clinical trial and is consistent with EGFR-addicted NSCLC biology.

2. EGFR T790M (VAF 22%) is a co-occurring acquired resistance mutation, now detected at substantially higher tissue VAF than on prior ctDNA analysis (1.6%). The co-occurrence of L858R and T790M in cis constitutes a resistant EGFR compound mutation. Although third-generation osi retains activity against T790M in the first-line setting, the emergence of T790M alongside high-level MET amplification under osi selective pressure defines a particularly resistant tumor profile.

3. MET amplification (CN 6.8, high-level) is the dominant acquired bypass resistance mechanism in this tumor. MET-driven bypass signaling sustains downstream oncogenic signaling despite EGFR pathway inhibition. This finding is directly targetable by the planned investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody and represents the primary molecular rationale for clinical trial enrollment. High-level MET amplification (CN >6) is a well-established predictor of benefit from MET-targeted therapy in this context.

4. RB1 p.Q670* (VAF 18%, newly detected) raises concern for emerging phenotypic plasticity and possible histologic transformation potential, although no small cell transformation was identified on current pathology. Continued clinical and radiographic surveillance is warranted.

5. TP53 p.R248W (VAF 52%) is a persistent, high-frequency truncal co-mutation associated with modestly adverse prognosis in EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

6. ARID1A p.E1814* (VAF 24%) remains a VUS with increasing clonal frequency; no immediately actionable implication.

Overall genomic profile: EGFR L858R/T790M co-mutation with high-level MET amplification as a combined, co-occurring acquired resistance mechanism, arising in the setting of prior osi and subsequent platinum-based chemotherapy. This genomic landscape is clinically consistent with the EGFR/MET co-targeting therapeutic hypothesis and supports enrollment on the planned investigational bispecific antibody clinical trial.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Yuen is well known to our thoracic oncology service. For context: she was initially diagnosed with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma harboring an EGFR exon 21 L858R point mutation approximately two years ago at age 62. At that time, mediastinal staging confirmed multi-station N2 involvement (stations 4R and 7) by PET-CT and EBUS-guided biopsy. She declined concurrent chemoradiation due to concerns about her underlying COPD (GOLD Stage I) and proceeded instead with upfront osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily, achieving a substantial partial response by 3 months with continued deepening through 9 months (primary mass down from 4.8 cm to 1.8 cm at nadir, mediastinal nodes normalized). She remained on osimertinib for approximately 14 months before developing radiographic progression with enlargement of the primary mass (back up to 3.2 cm), new right paratracheal adenopathy, a small right pleural effusion, and two new brain metastases (1.1 cm right frontal lobe, 0.8 cm right cerebellum).

At that time, liquid biopsy via Guardant360 demonstrated acquisition of an EGFR T790M mutation at low VAF (1.6%), and tissue re-biopsy was deferred initially. She underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to both brain lesions using the Varian Edge system (20 Gy in 1 fraction to the right frontal lesion, 22 Gy in 1 fraction to the cerebellar lesion), then transitioned to second-line systemic therapy with carboplatin (AUC 5) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2) plus bevacizumab (15 mg/kg) every 21 days. She completed 4 cycles with confirmed partial response systemically and intracranial treatment response post-SRS, then transitioned to maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab.

MRI perfusion and spectroscopy at that time favored true tumor recurrence over radiation necrosis (elevated rCBV, elevated choline:NAA ratio).

Maintenance chemotherapy was discontinued and she underwent repeat SRS to the right frontal lesion (18 Gy in 1 fraction, reduced dose given prior irradiation). ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Yuen is a 64-year-old woman with Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma with acquired osimertinib resistance mediated by concurrent EGFR T790M and MET amplification, having now completed one cycle of an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody combined with anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy on a Phase I/II clinical trial. She is presenting for her week 4 on-study safety and tolerability assessment.

Overall, her first cycle has been tolerated reasonably well. Key issues to address today are as follows:

1. CLINICAL TRIAL CONTINUATION AND TOLERABILITY -- Grade 1 infusion-related reaction during cycle 1 day 1 was the primary adverse event of note. This is a well-recognized reaction pattern with bispecific antibody constructs due to cytokine release. Going forward, enhanced pre-medication regimen has been added (dexamethasone 8 mg IV, diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, acetaminophen 650 mg PO) per protocol amendment, with a slower initial infusion rate. No dose modification is required at this time. She remains protocol-eligible. Cycle 2 is planned to proceed on schedule. The trial team will be notified of today's clinical summary and labs per required protocol communication. All grade 1 adverse events (fatigue, nausea, grade 1 infusion-related reaction) will be documented in the electronic study data capture system.

2. INTRACRANIAL STATUS AND HEADACHES -- The patient reports significant improvement in headaches following repeat SRS and completion of dexamethasone taper. The absence of new neurologic symptoms is reassuring. Dexamethasone taper is now complete, which is favorable from a glucose management perspective in the setting of her diabetes. Cycle 3 restaging MRI brain will provide the next formal intracranial assessment. If the right frontal lesion is stable or responding post-repeat SRS, we will continue to monitor on the standard brain MRI schedule. If there is concern for further enlargement at that time, neurosurgical consultation will be revisited. No interventions needed today for intracranial disease.

3. DYSPNEA -- Subjective improvement noted since initiating trial therapy. I am cautiously encouraged, but acknowledge that restaging imaging is the only reliable measure of pulmonary tumor response. Dedicated Staging Examination

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Yuen is a 62-year-old Chinese-American female with a newly diagnosed right upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma harboring an EGFR exon 21 L858R sensitizing mutation. Staging whole-body PET-CT demonstrated a hypermetabolic 4.8 cm primary mass with ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy (stations 4R and 7), classified as clinical Stage IIIB (T3N2M0). Brain was not included in the PET-CT field of view. This dedicated cranial MRI is obtained for CNS staging prior to initiation of systemic therapy, per institutional protocol for patients with locally advanced NSCLC harboring actionable driver mutations.

COMPARISON:
No prior brain imaging available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner using a 20-channel head coil. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1-weighted, axial T2-weighted FLAIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial gradient echo (GRE) susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), axial T2-weighted fast spin echo, and pre- and post-contrast axial and coronal T1-weighted volumetric sequences (MPRAGE). Gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadobutrol, 0.1 mmol/kg) was administered intravenously without adverse reaction. Total examination time: approximately 45 minutes.

FINDINGS:

BRAIN PARENCHYMA:
The cerebral hemispheres are symmetric in size and signal intensity. The cortical gray matter and underlying white matter demonstrate normal signal characteristics on all sequences. There is no abnormal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity within the supratentorial white matter to suggest infiltrative disease, treatment-related change, or demyelination, beyond a single punctate focus of T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the right posterior periventricular white matter, measuring less than 3 mm, without associated diffusion restriction or enhancement -- this is a nonspecific finding most consistent with a small vessel chronic ischemic change, as may be seen in a patient with long-standing hypertension and diabetes. No additional white matter signal abnormalities are identified.

FOCAL MASS LESIONS / METASTATIC DISEASE:
No discrete intra-axial mass lesion is identified within the supratentorial or infratentorial compartments. On post-contrast T1-weighted sequences, there is no abnormal focal parenchymal enhancement to suggest metastatic deposits anywhere within the cerebral hemispheres, cerebellum, or brainstem. Specifically, no enhancing nodule, ring-enhancing lesion, or focal area of restricted diffusion consistent with a metastatic implant is identified. The post-contrast images were reviewed at multiple windowing settings and the cerebellum was carefully scrutinized given its known predilection for early metastatic seeding from lung adenocarcinoma.

LEPTOMENINGEAL SPACES:
The leptomeninges are unremarkable in appearance. There is no abnormal leptomeningeal or pachymeningeal thickening or enhancement. No sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity or subarachnoid enhancement to suggest leptomeningeal carcinomatosis is identified.

CALVARIUM / SKULL BASE:
The bony calvarium and skull base are intact on the available sequences. No aggressive osseous lesion, cortical destruction, or focal marrow replacement signal abnormality is appreciated. The clivus, petrous apices, and sphenoid wings appear unremarkable.

VENTRICLES AND CISTERNS:
The lateral, third, and fourth ventricles are normal in size and configuration. There is no evidence of hydrocephalus. The cerebral aqueduct appears patent. The basal cisterns are open and symmetric.

MIDLINE STRUCTURES:
There is no midline shift. The corpus callosum is intact throughout its length on sagittal T1-weighted sequences. The pineal gland is unremarkable with no evidence of a pineal mass. The pituitary gland and infundibular stalk are normal in morphology and signal intensity; there is no sellar mass or abnormal enhancement.

CEREBELLUM AND BRAINSTEM:
The cerebellar hemispheres and vermis are normal in size and signal. The cerebellar folia are well-defined. The brainstem (midbrain, pons, and medulla) is unremarkable in appearance, with no focal signal abnormality or mass effect. The superior and inferior cerebellar peduncles are symmetric and normally myelinated in appearance.

VASCULAR / FLOW VOIDS:
Flow voids in the major intracranial arteries (supraclinoid internal carotid arteries, middle cerebral arteries, anterior cerebral arteries, basilar artery, and posterior cerebral arteries) are preserved bilaterally. No asymmetric signal dropout on SWI sequences to suggest intracranial hemorrhage or vascular malformation. Dural venous sinuses appear patent based on flow characteristics.

SELLA / CAVERNOUS SINUSES:
The sella turcica is of normal size. The pituitary gland measures approximately 5 mm in vertical height without focal hypointense or hyperintense lesion. No cavernous sinus mass or asymmetry is noted.

ORBITS:
The globes and optic nerves appear symmetric and unremarkable on the included sequences. No retrobulbar mass is seen.

INCIDENTAL FINDINGS:
As noted above, a single punctate focus of T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the right posterior periventricular white matter consistent with a chronic microvascular change. This is a common incidental finding in patients with hypertension and diabetes and is of no acute clinical significance.

IMPRESSION:

1. No intracranial metastatic disease identified. There are no enhancing parenchymal lesions, no restricted diffusion abnormalities, and no leptomeningeal or pachymeningeal enhancement to indicate CNS involvement by the patient's known right upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma.

2. No intracranial mass effect, midline shift, or hydrocephalus.

3. A single, subcentimeter punctate T2/FLAIR hyperintense focus in the right posterior periventricular white matter is a nonspecific incidental finding most consistent with a chronic small vessel ischemic change given the patient's known history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. No follow-up is required for this finding.

4. Otherwise normal brain MRI with gadolinium contrast enhancement.

These findings are consistent with absence of intracranial metastatic disease at initial staging. In the clinical context of a newly diagnosed Stage IIIB EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, the CNS is clear at baseline, which is an important reference point for future surveillance imaging.

SUMMARY OF CLINICALLY RELEVANT FINDINGS
========================================================================

ACTIONABLE / CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT ALTERATIONS:

  1. EGFR p.L858R (c.2573T>G), Exon 21
     Classification: SENSITIZING MUTATION -- TIER I (Level A Evidence)
     Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 42%
     Genomic coordinates: Chr7:55,259,515 T>G
     Interpretation: Classic EGFR exon 21 L858R activating point mutation. This is one of the two most common sensitizing EGFR mutations in non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma (along with exon 19 deletions), accounting for approximately 40-45% of EGFR-mutant NSCLC cases. This mutation confers established, high-level sensitivity to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) of all three generations (Tarceva/gefi, afat, osi). Third-generation osi (Tagrisso) is preferred first-line therapy based on FLAURA trial data demonstrating superior PFS and OS over first-generation TKIs in EGFR-mutant NSCLC, with additional CNS penetration benefit. The VAF of 42% is consistent with a clonal, driver mutation.

CO-MUTATIONS AND ADDITIONAL VARIANTS:

  2. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T), Exon 7
     Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC -- TIER II (oncogenic, co-mutation)
     Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 38%
     Interpretation: TP53 R248W is a well-characterized gain-of-function hotspot mutation in the DNA-binding domain of p53. It is among the most frequently identified co-mutations in EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, occurring in approximately 30-40% of cases. TP53 co-mutation with EGFR sensitizing mutations has been associated in some studies with modestly reduced duration of response to EGFR TKI therapy and may confer a higher risk of acquired resistance emergence; however, TP53 status alone does not alter the recommendation for first-line EGFR TKI therapy. This mutation is not targetable with currently approved agents.

  3. ARID1A p.E1814* (c.5440G>T), Exon 20
     Classification: VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
     Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 18%
     Interpretation: ARID1A encodes a subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex. Truncating/loss-of-function alterations in ARID1A are recurrently observed in multiple tumor types including lung adenocarcinoma. At a VAF of 18%, this variant may represent a subclonal alteration. Its functional and clinical significance in this context is uncertain. No approved targeted therapy is available specifically for ARID1A-deficient tumors in lung cancer at this time. This finding may have future clinical relevance if the field advances. Serial monitoring of VAF may provide insight into clonal dynamics.

  4. KDR p.V297I (c.889G>A), Exon 7
     Classification: VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
     Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22%
     Interpretation: KDR (also known as VEGFR2) encodes vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2. This missense variant (V297I) is in the extracellular ligand-binding domain. It has been observed in population databases and some tumor datasets, and its functional consequence is unclear. It is not classified as an activating or inactivating mutation based on current evidence. Clinical significance in the context of anti-angiogenic therapy (e.g., Avastin) is unknown.

GENOMIC INTERPRETATION AND CLINICAL CONTEXT
========================================================================
This comprehensive genomic profiling result from the primary right upper lobe adenocarcinoma identifies EGFR p.L858R as the primary oncogenic driver mutation (VAF 42%), with co-occurring TP53 p.R248W (VAF 38%) and subclonal variants of uncertain significance (ARID1A p.E1814*, KDR p.V297I). The genomic landscape is consistent with EGFR-driven pulmonary adenocarcinoma in a patient with a light-to-moderate smoking history.

Key prognostic and predictive features of this profile:
  - EGFR L858R is a Tier I actionable sensitizing mutation with high-level evidence for EGFR TKI benefit (FDA-approved: osi, Tarceva, Iressa, Gilotrif, Vizimpro, erlo + ramu).
  - Absence of co-occurring STK11 or KEAP1 mutations is a favorable feature; these co-mutations have been associated with reduced TKI efficacy and more aggressive disease biology in some analyses.
  - Absence of RB1 mutation at baseline is notable; RB1 loss is associated with small cell transformation risk, which can occur as a resistance mechanism to EGFR TKI therapy, particularly with prolonged TKI exposure.
  - TP53 co-mutation (R248W, gain-of-function hotspot) at a high VAF clonal level may be associated with modestly inferior TKI outcomes compared to TP53 wild-type EGFR-mutant NSCLC, but does not change initial treatment selection.
  - Low TMB and MSS status indicate low likelihood of benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy.
  - No baseline T790M, C797S, MET amplification, or ERBB2 amplification identified at initial diagnosis (these are common acquired resistance mechanisms to EGFR TKIs and will be important to monitor at time of progression).
  - Low-level CDK6 copy number gain is of uncertain significance and does not currently warrant targeted intervention.

**MDT DISCUSSION SUMMARY:**

Ms. Yuen was formally presented at the thoracic multidisciplinary tumor board with attendance from thoracic surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, interventional pulmonology, diagnostic radiology, and pathology.

Thoracic Surgery Assessment:
Dr. [Thoracic Surgery] reviewed the imaging in detail. Given the CT and PET-CT findings demonstrating multi-station ipsilateral mediastinal N2 disease (stations 4R and 7, both with FDG-avidity confirmed on PET and histologically confirmed at station 4R via EBUS), the tumor was deemed surgically unresectable at this time. Thoracic surgery deferred a surgical recommendation and will reassess if downstaging is achieved with systemic or combined modality therapy.

Radiation Oncology Assessment:
Dr. [Radiation Oncology] discussed the role of definitive concurrent chemoradiation (CRT) in unresectable Stage III NSCLC, which has historically been the standard of care, followed by durvalumab consolidation per the PACIFIC trial paradigm. However, the team acknowledged that this patient has an actionable EGFR L858R sensitizing mutation, which substantially alters the treatment decision landscape. It was noted that in EGFR-mutant Stage III NSCLC, the role of consolidation durvalumab post-CRT is less clear (the PACIFIC trial largely did not include patients with EGFR mutations in the analysis supporting durvalumab benefit), and the LAURA trial has demonstrated a significant progression-free survival benefit with osimertinib consolidation following definitive chemoradiation in EGFR-mutant Stage III NSCLC. Radiation oncology expressed willingness to plan definitive CRT to total doses of approximately 60-66 Gy with concurrent platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin/etoposide or carboplatin/paclitaxel), followed by osimertinib maintenance.

Medical Oncology Assessment (this service):
Given the pathologically confirmed EGFR L858R sensitizing mutation (a third-generation EGFR TKI sensitizing alteration), osimertinib (Tagrisso, 80 mg orally daily) is the systemic agent of choice. Two potential approaches were discussed:
1. Definitive concurrent chemoradiation followed by osimertinib consolidation/maintenance (LAURA trial-based approach, FDA-approved indication)
2. Upfront osimertinib monotherapy without concurrent chemoradiation, given that osimertinib achieves excellent systemic disease control in EGFR-sensitizing mutation-positive NSCLC

The favorable molecular profile (STK11 WT, KEAP1 WT) supports robust TKI response. The low PD-L1 TPS of 15% does not argue strongly for immunotherapy monotherapy, and given the EGFR mutation, anti-PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy would not be recommended as initial treatment per standard guidelines. The co-occurring TP53 R248W mutation is of interest; TP53 co-mutations in EGFR-mutant NSCLC can be associated with modestly shorter response duration, but this does not change the initial treatment recommendation.

Patient Preference Discussion:
Following MDT deliberation, the treatment recommendation was presented to Ms. Yuen in detail with her daughter in attendance. I spent approximately 45 minutes discussing:
- The diagnosis of Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma
- The unresectable nature of the disease due to multi-station N2 involvement
- The standard CRT followed by osimertinib approach (LAURA trial data)
- The role of upfront osimertinib monotherapy as an alternative
- The toxicity profile of concurrent chemoradiation, including risk of radiation pneumonitis (particularly relevant given her underlying COPD, even mild), esophagitis, fatigue, cytopenias, and potential long-term pulmonary fibrosis
- The toxicity profile of osimertinib monotherapy: primarily dermatologic (acneiform rash), GI (diarrhea), and paronychia; rare but serious risks including interstitial lung disease (ILD) and QTc prolongation
- The curability discussion: Stage IIIB disease is potentially curable with combined modality therapy; however, approximately 30-40% of patients with Stage III NSCLC achieve long-term disease control with current approaches

Ms. Yuen expressed significant concern regarding the toxicity of concurrent chemoradiation, particularly given her underlying COPD (GOLD Stage I) and fear of radiation-related pulmonary complications. She noted that her COPD, while mild, has already impacted her quality of life. After extensive shared decision-making and after being given time to discuss with her family, she declined concurrent chemoradiation and elected to proceed with upfront osimertinib monotherapy.

This decision was respected. We discussed that while the LAURA data supports CRT-then-osimertinib in this setting, the evidence base for upfront osimertinib in Stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC is also supported by retrospective and single-arm prospective data, and that osimertinib monotherapy has demonstrated high response rates in this molecular subset. We will monitor closely with regular cross-sectional imaging and will revisit the role of thoracic radiation if there is disease stabilization or response.

**ASSESSMENT:**

1. Stage IIIB (T3N2M0, AJCC 8th edition) right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant (exon 21 L858R, VAF 42%), PD-L1 TPS 15%, ALK-negative, ROS1-negative. Multi-station N2 disease (stations 4R and 7), unresectable per thoracic surgery. No evidence of distant metastases.

2. Underlying COPD (GOLD Stage I) -- mild, on tiotropium and albuterol PRN. FEV1 80% predicted. Patient's concern regarding CRT-related pulmonary toxicity in the context of underlying COPD is clinically reasonable and was a significant factor in treatment decision-making.

3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- well-controlled (HbA1c 7.1%); will require monitoring during treatment (corticosteroid use for any TKI toxicity management may transiently worsen glycemic control).

4. Hypertension -- well-controlled on lisinopril.

5. Mild anemia (Hgb 12.8 g/dL) -- likely anemia of chronic disease/inflammation; will monitor on treatment.

**PLAN:**

1. Initiate osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as first-line treatment for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma. Target start date: approximately 2 weeks from today to allow for treatment coordination, financial counseling, and patient preparation.

2. Prior to treatment initiation:
   - Baseline ECG to assess QTc interval (osimertinib can prolong QTc; baseline QTc reviewed today was 418 ms -- within normal limits)
   - Baseline ophthalmology evaluation given baseline dry eye history (patient reports occasional dry eyes at baseline)
   - Baseline echocardiogram given cardiac risk factors
   - Repeat LFTs, CBC, CMP prior to first dose

3. Financial counseling: Facilitate access to AstraZeneca patient support program (Tagrisso ACCESS program) given osimertinib's significant cost. Patient has insurance but copay assistance should be explored.

4. Patient education:

   - Instructed to call our nurse triage line for any skin changes, dyspnea, or new neurologic symptoms
   - Advised to avoid concurrent CYP3A4 strong inducers (e.g., rifampin, St. John's Wort)
   - Sun protection counseling given risk of EGFR TKI dermatologic toxicity

5. Restaging imaging: Plan CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 12 weeks after initiation of osimertinib (first restaging scan). MRI brain to be repeated at approximately 6 months unless symptoms develop sooner.

6. Continue current medications: lisinopril, metformin, tiotropium, albuterol PRN, atorvastatin, aspirin, vitamin D3. No drug-drug interactions of concern with osimertinib anticipated.

7. Diabetes management: Coordinate with primary care physician regarding glycemic monitoring during treatment, particularly if corticosteroids are needed for any toxicity management.

8. Palliative care: Will refer to supportive care/palliative care team for symptom management (cough, dyspnea) and psychosocial support, consistent with ASCO guidelines recommending early integration of palliative care in advanced lung cancer.

9. Social work referral: Placed today for assistance with financial toxicity, caregiver support, and community resources.

10. Return to clinic: Approximately 4 weeks after initiating osimertinib for first on-treatment assessment (symptom review, labs, toxicity assessment). The patient and her daughter were given my direct contact information and nurse triage line.

**Prognosis discussed:** I had an honest and compassionate discussion with Ms. Yuen and her daughter regarding the nature of Stage IIIB NSCLC. While cure is our shared goal, I was transparent that Stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC treated with EGFR TKI-based approaches has a favorable but heterogeneous trajectory. Median progression-free survival on osimertinib in Stage IV EGFR-mutant NSCLC is approximately 18-20 months (FLAURA data), and Stage III disease may behave differently. We discussed that the goal is long-term disease control and quality of life preservation. Psychosocial support and advance care planning discussions are available at any time.

CLINICAL INDICATION:

Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of nonionic iodinated intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350) with portal venous phase acquisition through the abdomen and pelvis. Chest images were acquired in the portal venous phase at 1.25 mm slice thickness reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. FINDINGS:

PELVIS:

IMPRESSION:

1.
